feynman.0: Here’s a curious thing: an object going round a circle at a perfectly steady speed is accelerating at every single instant — because its direction of motion keeps changing. And that turns the whole topic into one job: figure out which real force, pointing toward the centre, is doing the pushing.
feynman.1: An object is in uniform circular motion when it runs around a circle of fixed radius at constant speed. The speed stays put, but the velocity does not — and that’s the catch, because velocity is a vector and its direction is forever turning. At every point the velocity is tangential — it points along the circle, square to the radius. Since the direction keeps changing, the object is accelerating, even though its speed never budges.
feynman.2: The acceleration points straight at the centre of the circle. We call it the centripetal acceleration (“centre-seeking”), and for an object going at speed v around a circle of radius r its size is:
feynman.3: The acceleration is radial — perpendicular to the velocity at every instant. Now Newton’s second law won’t let a mass accelerate unless some unbalanced force is shoving it, so there has to be a net force pointing toward the centre. That net force is the centripetal force:
feynman.4: Don’t be fooled: “centripetal force” is not some new kind of force, and it never shows up as its own arrow in a free-body diagram. It’s just the name we hang on the resultant of the real forces — gravity, tension, friction, the normal force — once you’ve added them all up. So in every problem the first thing to do is hunt down which real force, or which component of a real force, points toward the centre and therefore delivers the mv²/r.
feynman.5: For a car driving round a flat circular road, that force is the friction between the tyres and the road, pointing horizontally toward the centre of the bend. For something whirled on a horizontal string, it’s the horizontal component of the tension in the string. In both cases the net force, the acceleration and the radius all line up along the same line, aimed at the centre.
feynman.6: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity is tangential, while the acceleration and the net force both point toward the centre, with sizes v²/r and mv²/r. The speed is constant; the velocity is not.
feynman.7: Trap There is no outward “centrifugal force” in this physics — none. Nothing pushes the object away from the centre. That outward feeling a passenger swears they feel is really the seat or door pushing them inward to keep them on the circle, plus their own inertia trying to carry them straight ahead. So never draw an outward arrow, and never set the centripetal force equal to some imagined outward force.
feynman.8: Connects The centripetal force is always perpendicular to the velocity, so it does zero work (work = force × displacement along the force). And that’s exactly why the speed in uniform circular motion stays put — the net force keeps swinging the direction of motion around, but it never touches the kinetic energy.
feynman.9: A 1200 kg car rounds a flat circular bend of radius 45 m at a constant speed of 15 m s⁻¹. Determine the magnitude of the friction force the road must provide, and the minimum coefficient of friction that makes this possible. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
feynman.10: Formula: friction is the only horizontal force going, so it has to supply the whole centripetal force, Fnet = mv²r.
feynman.12: Answer: Fnet = 6.0 × 10³ N, pointing horizontally toward the centre of the bend.
feynman.13: For the road to come up with this, the friction it has on hand, μmg, has to be at least 6.0 × 10³ N. On a flat road the normal force is N = mg = 1200 × 9.8 = 1.18 × 10⁴ N, so the smallest coefficient that works is μ = Fnet/N = 6.0 × 10³ ÷ 1.18 × 10⁴ = 0.51. If the friction actually available falls short of this, the car can’t hold the bend and slides off outward along a tangent.
feynman.14: A road or track is banked when its surface is tilted toward the centre of the bend. The whole point of banking is to let a vehicle round the bend without leaning on friction at all. On a frictionless banked surface there are only two forces on the vehicle: its weight mg, pointing straight down, and the normal force N, pointing perpendicular to the tilted surface. And because the surface is tilted by angle θ from the horizontal, the normal force is tilted by that same θ from the vertical.
feynman.15: Now break the normal force into horizontal and vertical pieces. The horizontal component, N sinθ, points toward the centre of the bend and supplies the centripetal force. The vertical component, N cosθ, points up and balances the weight, because there’s no vertical acceleration. Writing down both conditions:
feynman.16: Divide the first equation by the second and out goes N, and out goes m, since sinθ/cosθ = tanθ:
feynman.17: Rearrange that and you get the centripetal acceleration and the design speed — the one special speed at which a vehicle rounds the frictionless bank perfectly:
feynman.18: At the design speed the answer leans only on r, g and θ — the mass of the vehicle drops out entirely. A truck and a motorcycle take the same frictionless bank at the very same design speed.
feynman.19: Key idea On a frictionless banked track the horizontal component of the normal force, N sinθ, supplies the centripetal force all on its own, while the vertical component N cosθ balances the weight. That gives ac = g tanθ and the design speed v = √(rg tanθ).
feynman.20: Trap The normal force on a slope is not equal to mg — watch out for that. On a bank it’s N = mg/cosθ, which is bigger than mg. Resolve N into horizontal and vertical components and write a separate equation for each direction; don’t just assume the normal force balances the weight head-on. At the design speed there’s no friction — only the components of N are doing anything. Go above the design speed and the vehicle tends to slide up the bank, so friction points down the slope; go below it and the vehicle tends to slide down, so friction points up the slope.
feynman.21: A test track is banked at 20° to the horizontal. A bend on the track has radius 80 m. Determine the design speed at which a vehicle rounds this bend with no reliance on friction, and the centripetal acceleration at that speed. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
feynman.22: Formula: at the design speed the horizontal component of the normal force supplies the centripetal force all by itself, which gives v = √(rg tanθ) and ac = g tanθ.
feynman.23: Substitution: ac = 9.8 × tan20° = 9.8 × 0.364 = 3.57 m s⁻², and v = √(80 × 9.8 × tan20°) = √(80 × 9.8 × 0.364) = √285
feynman.24: Answer: v = 16.9 m s⁻¹ ≈ 17 m s⁻¹, with a centripetal acceleration of 3.6 m s⁻² pointing horizontally toward the centre of the bend. Notice the mass of the vehicle never showed up in the calculation.
feynman.25: When an object goes round a vertical circle — a ball on a string, a bucket of water swung overhead, a car cresting a hump or dropping through a valley — gravity behaves the same way all the way round (straight down), but its relationship to the centre of the circle shifts from point to point. We only have to do the analysis at the two extreme points, the highest point and the lowest point, where the net force, the weight and the tension (or normal force) all lie along the vertical line through the centre.
feynman.26: At the top of the circle the centre is directly below the object. Both the weight mg and the string tension T point downward — toward the centre — so they team up and add. Newton’s second law toward the centre gives:
feynman.27: At the bottom of the circle the centre is directly above the object. Now the tension points up toward the centre while the weight points down away from it, so this time they fight and subtract. Taking toward the centre (upward) as positive:
feynman.28: So the tension at the bottom comes out bigger than at the top for the same speed, because down there the tension has two jobs at once: it has to hold up the weight and supply the centripetal force.
feynman.29: As the object slows down near the top, it needs less and less tension. The minimum speed at the top is the speed where the string just goes slack — the tension drops to zero and gravity alone is left to supply the centripetal force. Setting T = 0 in the top equation:
feynman.30: Go any slower than this and the object can’t keep to the circular path at the top — it falls inward. For a vehicle the supporting surface gives a normal force N instead of tension. Crossing a hump (a convex crest), the centre is below the vehicle, so mg − N = mv²/r, which gives N = m(g − v²/r): the road pushes up less than the weight, and the driver feels lighter. In a dip (a concave valley) the centre is above, so N − mg = mv²/r, giving N = m(g + v²/r): the road pushes up harder than the weight, and the driver feels heavier. The normal force falls to zero, and the vehicle leaves the road, when v² = gr over the crest.
feynman.31: Key idea At the top of a vertical circle both T and mg point toward the centre, so T + mg = mv²/r. At the bottom they oppose each other, so T − mg = mv²/r. The string just goes slack at the top when v = √(gr).
feynman.32: Trap The sign of the weight flips between the two points because the centre is in a different direction, not because gravity changes — gravity is always mg straight down, no matter what. At the top “down” happens to be toward the centre, so the weight adds to the tension; at the bottom “down” is away from the centre, so the weight subtracts. Always settle which way the centre lies before you write Fnet = mv²/r, and never assume the minimum-speed condition applies anywhere except the top.
feynman.33: A 0.20 kg ball on the end of a string of length 0.80 m is swung in a vertical circle. Determine the minimum speed the ball can have at the highest point while the string stays taut, and the string tension at the highest point when the ball moves there at 4.0 m s⁻¹. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
feynman.34: Formula (minimum speed): at the top the string is right on the edge of slack, so T = 0 and mg = mv²/r, which gives vmin = √(gr).
feynman.36: Answer: vmin = 2.8 m s⁻¹. Any slower than this and the string goes slack and the ball falls inward.
feynman.37: Formula (tension at 4.0 m s⁻¹): at the top both forces point toward the centre, so T + mg = mv²/r, which rearranges to T = mv²r − mg.
feynman.39: Answer: T = 2.0 N, pointing along the string toward the centre. Because 4.0 m s⁻¹ beats the minimum speed of 2.8 m s⁻¹, the tension comes out positive and the string stays taut.
feynman.40: A satellite — whether a natural one like the Moon or an artificial one like the International Space Station — can be modelled as an object in uniform circular motion. It runs around the central body at constant speed on a path of fixed radius, so everything we worked out for circular motion carries straight over: the velocity is tangential, and the acceleration of size v²/r points toward the centre of the orbit. The one force that supplies this centripetal acceleration is gravity — but the quantitative link between that force and the orbit, namely Newton’s law of universal gravitation and the relationship between orbital radius and period, belongs to Area of Study 2 and gets developed in the Fields chapter. Here we treat the satellite purely as a case of circular motion.
feynman.41: The radius you plug into Fnet = mv²/r and a = v²/r is the orbital radius measured from the centre of the central body, in metres:
feynman.42: The orbit is independent of the satellite’s mass. Both a = v²/r and the gravitational acceleration depend only on the orbital radius and the central mass, so two satellites of different mass sharing the same orbit have the same speed, the same period and the same acceleration. A spanner let go by an astronaut just keeps pace with the station right beside it.
feynman.43: A satellite in a circular orbit is accelerating, even with its speed dead constant, because its direction of motion is forever changing. The acceleration is explained by Newton’s second law: an unbalanced force — gravity — acts toward the centre, and an unbalanced force makes an acceleration in its own direction. It is not explained by the first law (which is about motion when forces are balanced) or the third law (which is about the equal and opposite force the satellite exerts back on Earth).
feynman.44: Key idea Model a satellite as uniform circular motion of radius r = RE + altitude (in metres). The orbit is independent of the satellite’s mass. The satellite accelerates because an unbalanced force — gravity — acts toward the centre: this is Newton’s second law.
feynman.45: Trap Two mark-losers that keep coming back. First, the orbital radius is not the altitude: an altitude of 400 km gives r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 4.00 × 10⁵ = 6.77 × 10⁶ m, not 4.00 × 10⁵ m. Always add RE and convert the altitude from km to m. Second, “why is a satellite in a circular orbit accelerating?” is answered by Newton’s second law — an unbalanced (net) force causes acceleration toward the centre — never the first or third law.
feynman.46: Connects The reason gravity supplies exactly mv²/r, and how the orbital radius pins down the period, comes from Newton’s law of universal gravitation in Area of Study 2. The full orbit derivation, geostationary satellites and apparent weightlessness are all developed there — see Satellites in circular orbit in the Fields chapter.
feynman.47: The International Space Station orbits at an altitude of 400 km with an orbital period of 92 min. Modelling its motion as uniform circular motion, determine the orbital radius, the orbital speed and the centripetal acceleration. Take RE = 6.37 × 10⁶ m.
feynman.48: Formula: r = RE + altitude, then v = 2πrT and a = v²r.
feynman.49: Substitution (radius): r = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 4.00 × 10⁵ = 6.77 × 10⁶ m, with the altitude converted from 400 km to 4.00 × 10⁵ m. The period is T = 92 × 60 = 5.52 × 10³ s.
feynman.51: Answer: a = v²/r = (7.71 × 10³)² ÷ (6.77 × 10⁶) = 8.8 m s⁻², pointing toward Earth’s centre. And here’s the nice part: this equals the gravitational field strength at that altitude, which is exactly why gravity alone keeps the orbit going — and the result doesn’t depend one bit on the station’s mass.

sagan.1: An object moves in uniform circular motion when it travels around a circle of fixed radius at constant speed. The speed holds steady, yet the velocity does not — for velocity is a vector, and its direction wheels around without pause. At every point along the path the velocity is tangential, lying along the circle, perpendicular to the radius that reaches out from the centre. And because that direction is forever changing, the object is accelerating, even though its speed never wavers.
sagan.2: The acceleration points inward, toward the very centre of the circle. We call it the centripetal acceleration — the “centre-seeking” acceleration — and for an object of speed v sweeping around a circle of radius r, its magnitude is:
sagan.3: The acceleration is radial, standing perpendicular to the velocity at every instant. And by Newton’s second law a mass cannot accelerate unless some unbalanced force lays hold of it, so a net force must reach toward the centre. This net force we name the centripetal force:
sagan.4: “Centripetal force” is not some new and separate force, and it never appears in a free-body diagram as an arrow of its own. It is simply the name we give to the sum of the real forces — gravity, tension, friction, the normal force — once they have been gathered together. In every problem the first task is to discover which real force, or which component of a real force, points toward the centre and so provides mv²/r.
sagan.5: For a vehicle travelling around a flat circular road, that force is friction between the tyres and the road, reaching horizontally toward the centre of the bend. In each case the net force, the acceleration and the radius all lie along the same line, all pointing inward toward the centre.
sagan.6: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity is tangential, while the acceleration and the net force both reach toward the centre, with magnitude v²/r and mv²/r. The speed is constant; the velocity is not.
sagan.7: Trap There is no outward “centrifugal force” in this physics. Nothing whatever pushes the object away from the centre. The outward feeling a passenger reports is the seat or the door pressing them inward, holding them to the circle, together with their own inertia carrying them onward in a straight line. Never draw an outward arrow, and never set the centripetal force equal to some imagined outward force.
sagan.8: Connects The centripetal force stands forever perpendicular to the velocity, and so it does zero work (work = force × displacement along the force). That is precisely why the speed in uniform circular motion never changes — the net force turns the direction of motion endlessly, yet never adds to or takes from the kinetic energy.

The vertical component, N cosθ, points upward and balances the weight, for there is no vertical acceleration. Writing both conditions:

tyson.10: Formula: friction is the only horizontal force in town, so it supplies the centripetal force, Fnet = mv²r.
tyson.12: Answer: Fnet = 6.0 × 10³ N, directed horizontally toward the centre of the bend.
tyson.13: For the road to deliver that, the available friction μmg has to be at least 6.0 × 10³ N. On a flat road the normal force is N = mg = 1200 × 9.8 = 1.18 × 10⁴ N, so the minimum coefficient is μ = Fnet/N = 6.0 × 10³ ÷ 1.18 × 10⁴ = 0.51. If the friction on offer falls short of that, the car can’t hold the bend — it slides outward along a tangent.
tyson.14: A road or track is banked when its surface is tilted toward the centre of the bend. That tilt lets a vehicle round the bend without leaning on friction at all. On a frictionless banked surface there are only two forces on the vehicle: its weight mg, pointing straight down, and the normal force N, pointing perpendicular to the tilted surface. And because the surface is tilted by angle θ from the horizontal, the normal force is tilted by that same θ from the vertical.
tyson.15: Break the normal force into horizontal and vertical components and watch what each one does. The horizontal component, N sinθ, points toward the centre of the bend — that’s your centripetal force. The vertical component, N cosθ, points up and balances the weight, since there’s no vertical acceleration. Writing both conditions:
tyson.16: Divide the first equation by the second — out go N and m, since sinθ/cosθ = tanθ:
tyson.17: Rearrange and you land on the centripetal acceleration and the design speed — the one and only speed at which a vehicle takes the frictionless bank flawlessly:
tyson.18: At the design speed the answer rides on r, g and θ — and nothing else. The mass of the vehicle simply doesn’t enter. A truck and a motorcycle take the same frictionless bank at the very same design speed.
tyson.19: Key idea On a frictionless banked track the horizontal component of the normal force, N sinθ, supplies the centripetal force all on its own, while the vertical component N cosθ balances the weight. That hands you ac = g tanθ and the design speed v = √(rg tanθ).
tyson.20: Trap The normal force on a slope is not equal to mg — resist the urge. On a bank it’s N = mg/cosθ, which is bigger than mg. Resolve N into horizontal and vertical components and write a separate equation for each direction; don’t just assume the normal force balances the weight head-on. At the design speed there’s no friction at all — only the components of N are doing anything. Push above the design speed and the vehicle tends to slide up the bank, so friction points down the slope; drop below it and the vehicle tends to slide down, so friction points up the slope.
tyson.21: A test track is banked at 20° to the horizontal. A bend on the track has radius 80 m. Determine the design speed at which a vehicle rounds this bend with no reliance on friction, and the centripetal acceleration at that speed. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
tyson.22: Formula: at the design speed the horizontal component of the normal force supplies the centripetal force single-handed, giving v = √(rg tanθ) and ac = g tanθ.
tyson.23: Substitution: ac = 9.8 × tan20° = 9.8 × 0.364 = 3.57 m s⁻², and v = √(80 × 9.8 × tan20°) = √(80 × 9.8 × 0.364) = √285
tyson.24: Answer: v = 16.9 m s⁻¹ ≈ 17 m s⁻¹, with a centripetal acceleration of 3.6 m s⁻² directed horizontally toward the centre of the bend. The mass of the vehicle never shows up in the calculation.
tyson.25: When an object runs a vertical circle — a ball on a string, a bucket of water swung overhead, a vehicle cresting a hump or plunging through a valley — gravity behaves the same way the whole time (straight down), but its relationship to the centre of the circle flips from point to point. You only need to crack the analysis at the two extreme spots, the highest point and the lowest point, where the net force, the weight and the tension (or normal force) all sit along the vertical line through the centre.
tyson.26: At the top of the circle the centre sits directly below the object. Both the weight mg and the string tension T point downward — toward the centre — so they pile on together. Newton’s second law toward the centre gives:
tyson.27: At the bottom of the circle the centre is directly above the object. Now the tension points up toward the centre while the weight points down away from it, so they fight — they subtract. Taking toward the centre (upward) as positive:
tyson.28: So the tension at the bottom beats the tension at the top for the same speed, because down there the tension has two jobs — it must hold up the weight and supply the centripetal force.
tyson.29: As the object slows near the top, it needs less tension. The minimum speed at the top is the speed where the string just goes slack — tension drops to zero and gravity alone carries the whole centripetal load. Set T = 0 in the top equation:
tyson.30: Go any slower than that and the object can’t hold the circular path at the top — it falls inward. For a vehicle the supporting surface supplies a normal force N in place of tension. Crest a hump (a convex rise) and the centre sits below the vehicle, so mg − N = mv²/r, giving N = m(g − v²/r): the road pushes up less than the weight, and the driver feels lighter. Drop through a dip (a concave valley) and the centre sits above, so N − mg = mv²/r, giving N = m(g + v²/r): the road pushes up harder than the weight, and the driver feels heavier. The normal force falls to zero, and the vehicle leaves the road, when v² = gr over the crest.
tyson.31: Key idea At the top of a vertical circle both T and mg point toward the centre, so T + mg = mv²/r. At the bottom they oppose, so T − mg = mv²/r. The string just goes slack at the top when v = √(gr).
tyson.32: Trap The sign of the weight flips between the two points because the centre lies in a different direction — not because gravity changes its mind. Gravity is always mg straight down, end of story. At the top “down” is toward the centre, so the weight adds to the tension; at the bottom “down” is away from the centre, so the weight subtracts. Always decide which way the centre lies before you write Fnet = mv²/r, and never assume the minimum-speed condition shows up anywhere but the top.
tyson.33: A 0.20 kg ball on the end of a string of length 0.80 m is swung in a vertical circle. Determine the minimum speed the ball can have at the highest point while the string stays taut, and the string tension at the highest point when the ball moves there at 4.0 m s⁻¹. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
tyson.34: Formula (minimum speed): at the top the string is right on the edge of going slack, so T = 0 and mg = mv²/r, giving vmin = √(gr).
tyson.36: Answer: vmin = 2.8 m s⁻¹. Drop below this speed and the string goes slack — the ball falls inward.
tyson.37: Formula (tension at 4.0 m s⁻¹): at the top both forces point toward the centre, so T + mg = mv²/r, giving T = mv²r − mg.
tyson.39: Answer: T = 2.0 N, directed along the string toward the centre. Because 4.0 m s⁻¹ clears the minimum speed of 2.8 m s⁻¹, the tension comes out positive and the string stays taut.
tyson.40: A satellite — whether it’s a natural one like the Moon or an artificial one like the International Space Station — can be modelled as an object in uniform circular motion. It loops around the central body at constant speed on a path of fixed radius, so everything we nailed down for circular motion carries straight over: the velocity is tangential, and the acceleration of magnitude v²/r points toward the centre of the orbit. The single force supplying that centripetal acceleration is gravity — but the quantitative link between that force and the orbit — Newton’s law of universal gravitation and the relationship between orbital radius and period — belongs to Area of Study 2 and gets built up in the Fields chapter. Here we treat the satellite as nothing more than a case of circular motion.
tyson.41: The radius you plug into Fnet = mv²/r and a = v²/r is the orbital radius measured from the centre of the central body, in metres:
tyson.42: The orbit is independent of the satellite’s mass. Both a = v²/r and the gravitational acceleration ride only on the orbital radius and the central mass, so two satellites of different mass sharing the same orbit have the same speed, the same period and the same acceleration. A spanner an astronaut lets go keeps perfect pace with the station right beside it.
tyson.43: A satellite in a circular orbit is accelerating — even with its speed locked constant — because its direction of motion is forever changing. And the explanation is Newton’s second law: an unbalanced force — gravity — acts toward the centre, and an unbalanced force produces an acceleration in its own direction. It is not the first law (which handles motion when forces are balanced) and not the third law (which is about the equal and opposite force the satellite exerts back on Earth).
tyson.44: Key idea Model a satellite as uniform circular motion of radius r = RE + altitude (in metres). The orbit is independent of the satellite’s mass. The satellite accelerates because an unbalanced force — gravity — acts toward the centre: that’s Newton’s second law.
tyson.45: Trap Two recurring mark-killers. First, the orbital radius is not the altitude: an altitude of 400 km gives r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 4.00 × 10⁵ = 6.77 × 10⁶ m, not 4.00 × 10⁵ m. Always add RE and convert the altitude from km to m. Second, “why is a satellite in a circular orbit accelerating?” is answered by Newton’s second law — an unbalanced (net) force drives acceleration toward the centre — never the first or third law.
tyson.46: Connects Why gravity supplies exactly mv²/r, and how the orbital radius pins down the period, comes out of Newton’s law of universal gravitation in Area of Study 2. The full orbit derivation, geostationary satellites and apparent weightlessness all get developed there — see Satellites in circular orbit in the Fields chapter.
tyson.47: The International Space Station orbits at an altitude of 400 km with an orbital period of 92 min. Modelling its motion as uniform circular motion, determine the orbital radius, the orbital speed and the centripetal acceleration. Take RE = 6.37 × 10⁶ m.
tyson.48: Formula: r = RE + altitude, then v = 2πrT and a = v²r.
tyson.49: Substitution (radius): r = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 4.00 × 10⁵ = 6.77 × 10⁶ m, with the altitude converted from 400 km to 4.00 × 10⁵ m. The period is T = 92 × 60 = 5.52 × 10³ s.
tyson.51: Answer: a = v²/r = (7.71 × 10³)² ÷ (6.77 × 10⁶) = 8.8 m s⁻², directed toward Earth’s centre. That matches the gravitational field strength at that altitude — which is precisely why gravity alone keeps the orbit going — and the result doesn’t depend one bit on the station’s mass.

Because the velocity is always changing direction, the object is accelerating, even though its speed never changes. The distinction is exact, and worth holding onto.

cox.25: When an object moves in a vertical circle — a ball on a string, a bucket of water swung overhead, a vehicle cresting a hump or dipping through a valley — gravity behaves exactly the same way throughout, pulling straight down. cox.26: At the top of the circle the centre lies directly below the object. Both the weight mg and the string tension T point downward — toward the centre — so they add together. Newton’s second law toward the centre then gives:
cox.27: At the bottom of the circle the centre lies directly above the object. Now the tension points upward toward the centre while the weight points downward, away from it, so this time they subtract. Taking toward the centre (upward) as positive:

cox.30: Slower than this and the object simply can’t hold the circular path at the top — it falls inward. For a vehicle the supporting surface provides a normal force N in place of tension. cox.33: A 0.20 kg ball on the end of a string of length 0.80 m is swung in a vertical circle. Determine the minimum speed the ball can have at the highest point while the string stays taut, and the string tension at the highest point when the ball moves there at 4.0 m s⁻¹. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².

cox.37: Formula (tension at 4.0 m s⁻¹): at the top both forces point toward the centre, so T + mg = mv²/r, giving T = mv²r − mg.

cox.40: A satellite — whether a natural one such as the Moon or an artificial one such as the International Space Station — can be modelled, beautifully simply, as an object in uniform circular motion. cox.42: And the orbit is independent of the satellite’s mass. cox.43: A satellite in a circular orbit is accelerating, even though its speed holds steady, because its direction of motion is forever changing. cox.47: The International Space Station orbits at an altitude of 400 km with an orbital period of 92 min. Modelling its motion as uniform circular motion, determine the orbital radius, the orbital speed and the centripetal acceleration. Take RE = 6.37 × 10⁶ m.

Velocity is a vector, and its direction is forever turning. At every point along the path the velocity is tangential — laid out along the circle, perpendicular to the radius. And because that direction is always changing, the object is accelerating, even though its speed never wavers.
greene.2: Which way does this acceleration point? Toward the centre of the circle, always. We call it the centripetal acceleration (“centre-seeking”), and for an object of speed v moving on a circle of radius r its magnitude is:
greene.3: The acceleration is radial — perpendicular to the velocity at every instant. Now bring in Newton: a mass cannot accelerate unless an unbalanced force pushes on it, so something must be pulling toward the centre. That something is the centripetal force:
greene.4: Here is the point that trips so many up. “Centripetal force” is not a new kind of force, and it never appears in a free-body diagram as an arrow of its own. It is simply the name we give to the resultant of the real forces — gravity, tension, friction, the normal force — once you add them all together. So in every problem the first task is always the same: identify which real force, or which component of a real force, points toward the centre and therefore supplies mv²/r.
greene.5: For a vehicle driving around a flat circular road, that force is friction between the tyres and the road, pointing horizontally toward the centre of the bend. For an object whirled on a horizontal string, it is the horizontal component of the tension in the string. In each case the net force, the acceleration and the radius all lie along the same line, aimed at the centre.

greene.7: Trap There is no outward “centrifugal force” in this physics. Nothing pushes the object away from the centre. That outward feeling a passenger reports is really the seat or door pushing them inward to keep them on the circle, together with their own inertia carrying them straight on. Never draw an outward force arrow, and never set the centripetal force equal to an imagined outward one.
greene.8: Connects The centripetal force is always perpendicular to the velocity, so it does zero work (work = force × displacement along the force). And that is precisely why the speed in uniform circular motion holds steady — the net force forever changes the direction of motion, but never the kinetic energy.

hawking.33: A 0.20 kg ball on the end of a string of length 0.80 m is swung in a vertical circle. Determine the minimum speed the ball can have at the highest point while the string stays taut, and the string tension at the highest point when the ball moves there at 4.0 m s⁻¹. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².

hawking.40: A satellite — a natural one such as the Moon, or an artificial one such as the International Space Station — can be modelled as an object in uniform circular motion. hawking.47: The International Space Station orbits at an altitude of 400 km with an orbital period of 92 min. Modelling its motion as uniform circular motion, determine the orbital radius, the orbital speed and the centripetal acceleration. Take RE = 6.37 × 10⁶ m.

nye.0: Here’s a wild one! An object cruising at constant speed around a circle is accelerating at every single instant — and why? Because its direction of motion is always changing! The whole topic boils down to one job: spot which real force, pointing toward the centre, is supplying that acceleration.
nye.1: Check it out: an object moves in uniform circular motion when it travels around a circle of fixed radius at constant speed. The speed stays put — but the velocity does not! Velocity is a vector, and its direction keeps changing, moment to moment. At every point the velocity is tangential — pointing along the circle, square at right angles to the radius. And because that velocity is forever changing direction, the object is accelerating — even though its speed never budges!
nye.2: So where does this acceleration point? Straight toward the centre of the circle! We call it the centripetal acceleration (that means “centre-seeking”), and for an object zooming at speed v around a circle of radius r, here’s how big it is:
nye.3: This acceleration is radial — perpendicular to the velocity at every instant. Now here’s the thing: by Newton’s second law, a mass simply can’t accelerate unless an unbalanced force is shoving on it — so a net force has to point toward the centre. We call this net force the centripetal force:
nye.4: Now don’t get fooled — “centripetal force” is not some brand-new kind of force, and it never shows up in a free-body diagram as its own separate arrow! It’s just the name we give to the resultant of the real forces — gravity, tension, friction, the normal force — once you add them all up. So in every problem your very first job is to figure out which real force, or which component of a real force, points toward the centre and therefore supplies mv²/r.
nye.5: For a vehicle driving around a flat circular road, that force is friction between the tyres and the road, pointing horizontally toward the centre of the bend. For an object whirled on a horizontal string, it’s the horizontal component of the tension in the string. And get this — in each case the net force, the acceleration and the radius all line up along the same line, toward the centre!
nye.6: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity is tangential, while the acceleration and the net force both point toward the centre, with magnitude v²/r and mv²/r. The speed stays constant — the velocity does not!
nye.7: Trap Here’s a biggie — there is no outward “centrifugal force” in this physics! Nothing is pushing the object away from the centre. That outward feeling a passenger swears they feel? That’s actually the seat or the door pushing them inward to keep them on the circle, plus their own inertia trying to carry them straight on. So never draw an outward force arrow, and never set the centripetal force equal to some imagined outward force!
nye.8: Connects Here’s a neat one: the centripetal force is always perpendicular to the velocity, which means it does zero work (remember, work = force × displacement along the force). And that’s exactly why the speed in uniform circular motion stays constant — the net force changes the direction of motion, but never the kinetic energy!
nye.9: A 1200 kg car rounds a flat circular bend of radius 45 m at a constant speed of 15 m s⁻¹. Determine the magnitude of the friction force the road must provide, and the minimum coefficient of friction that makes this possible. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
nye.10: Formula: friction is the only horizontal force here, so it supplies the whole centripetal force, Fnet = mv²r.
nye.12: Answer: Fnet = 6.0 × 10³ N, directed horizontally toward the centre of the bend.
nye.13: For the road to pull this off, the available friction μmg has to be at least 6.0 × 10³ N. On a flat road the normal force is N = mg = 1200 × 9.8 = 1.18 × 10⁴ N, so the minimum coefficient is μ = Fnet/N = 6.0 × 10³ ÷ 1.18 × 10⁴ = 0.51. And if the actual friction available is any less than this? The car can’t hold the bend — it slides outward along a tangent!
nye.14: A road or track is banked when its surface is tilted toward the centre of the bend. Banking is a clever trick — it lets a vehicle round the bend without leaning on friction at all! On a frictionless banked surface, only two forces act on the vehicle: its weight mg, pointing straight down, and the normal force N, pointing perpendicular to the tilted surface. And because the surface is tilted by angle θ to the horizontal, the normal force is tilted by that same θ from the vertical.
nye.15: Now watch — let’s break the normal force into horizontal and vertical components. The horizontal component, N sinθ, points toward the centre of the bend and supplies the centripetal force. The vertical component, N cosθ, points upward and balances the weight, since there’s no vertical acceleration. Writing both conditions down:
nye.16: Now here’s the slick part — divide the first equation by the second and watch N and m vanish, since sinθ/cosθ = tanθ:
nye.17: Rearrange that and out pops the centripetal acceleration and the design speed — the one single speed at which a vehicle rounds the frictionless bank absolutely perfectly:
nye.18: And get this — at the design speed the answer depends only on r, g and θ — never on the mass of the vehicle! A truck and a motorcycle take the same frictionless bank at the exact same design speed. How cool is that?

nye.20: Trap Don’t get caught here — the normal force on a slope is not equal to mg! On a bank it’s N = mg/cosθ, which is bigger than mg. So resolve N into horizontal and vertical components and write a separate equation for each direction — don’t go assuming the normal force balances the weight directly. At the design speed there’s no friction at work — only the components of N do the job. Go above the design speed and the vehicle tends to slide up the bank, so friction points down the slope; drop below it and the vehicle tends to slide down, so friction points up the slope.

nye.25: Now let’s swing things into a vertical circle — a ball on a string, a bucket of water whirled overhead, a car cresting a hump or diving through a valley. It is not the first law (which is about motion when the forces are balanced) and not the third law (which is about the equal and opposite force the satellite exerts back on Earth).
munroe.44: Key idea Model a satellite as uniform circular motion of radius r = RE + altitude (in metres). The orbit is independent of the satellite’s mass. The satellite accelerates because an unbalanced force — gravity — acts toward the centre: this is Newton’s second law.
munroe.45: Trap Two things that keep costing people marks. First, the orbital radius is not the altitude: an altitude of 400 km gives r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 4.00 × 10⁵ = 6.77 × 10⁶ m, not 4.00 × 10⁵ m. Always add RE and convert the altitude from km to m. Second, “why is a satellite in a circular orbit accelerating?” is answered by Newton’s second law — an unbalanced (net) force causes acceleration toward the centre — never the first or third law.
munroe.46: Connects Why gravity supplies exactly mv²/r, and how the orbital radius pins down the period, comes out of Newton’s law of universal gravitation in Area of Study 2. The full orbit derivation, geostationary satellites and apparent weightlessness are all built up there — see Satellites in circular orbit in the Fields chapter.
munroe.47: The International Space Station orbits at an altitude of 400 km with an orbital period of 92 min. Modelling its motion as uniform circular motion, determine the orbital radius, the orbital speed and the centripetal acceleration. Take RE = 6.37 × 10⁶ m.
munroe.48: Formula: r = RE + altitude, then v = 2πrT and a = v²r.
munroe.49: Substitution (radius): r = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 4.00 × 10⁵ = 6.77 × 10⁶ m, with the altitude converted from 400 km to 4.00 × 10⁵ m. The period is T = 92 × 60 = 5.52 × 10³ s.
munroe.51: Answer: a = v²/r = (7.71 × 10³)² ÷ (6.77 × 10⁶) = 8.8 m s⁻², directed toward Earth’s centre. This is the same as the gravitational field strength at that altitude, which is exactly why gravity by itself keeps the orbit going — and the answer does not depend on the station’s mass.

bronowski.0: Consider an object carried at unchanging speed around a circle. It is, at every instant, accelerating — not because it hurries or slackens, but because the direction in which it moves is forever turning. And so the whole of this topic collapses into a single question, asked again and again: which real force, pointing toward the centre, is doing the turning?
bronowski.1: An object moves in uniform circular motion when it travels around a circle of fixed radius at a steady speed. The speed holds constant; the velocity does not — for velocity is a vector, and its direction is changing without pause. At each point the velocity lies tangential to the path, along the circle, square to the radius. And because that direction never stops changing, the object is accelerating, though its speed never alters at all.
bronowski.2: The acceleration is aimed at the centre of the circle. We call it the centripetal acceleration — the “centre-seeking” acceleration — and for an object of speed v on a circle of radius r its magnitude is:
bronowski.3: The acceleration is radial, set at right angles to the velocity at every instant. Now Newton teaches us that a mass cannot accelerate of its own accord; some unbalanced force must act upon it. So a net force must point toward the centre, and we name it the centripetal force:
bronowski.4: Here is a thing worth grasping firmly: “centripetal force” is not some new species of force, and it never appears in a free-body diagram as an arrow of its own. It is merely the name we give to the resultant of the real forces — gravity, tension, friction, the normal force — once they have been gathered and added. In every problem, then, the first task is to find which real force, or which component of a real force, points toward the centre and so provides mv²/r.
bronowski.5: For a vehicle driving around a flat circular road, that force is the friction between the tyres and the road, drawn horizontally toward the centre of the bend. For an object whirled on a horizontal string, it is the horizontal component of the tension in the string. In each case the net force, the acceleration and the radius lie along one and the same line, pointing toward the centre.
bronowski.6: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity runs forever tangential, while the acceleration and the net force both point inward, toward the centre, of magnitude v²/r and mv²/r. The speed is held constant; the velocity, changing its direction without pause, is not.
bronowski.7: Trap There is, in this physics, no outward “centrifugal force”. Nothing pushes the object away from the centre. The outward sensation a passenger reports is in truth the seat or the door pressing them inward, holding them to the circle, together with their own inertia carrying them straight on. Never draw an outward arrow, and never set the centripetal force equal to some imagined outward force.
bronowski.8: Connects The centripetal force stands always perpendicular to the velocity, and so it does zero work (work = force × displacement along the force). That is precisely why the speed in uniform circular motion holds constant — the net force turns the direction of motion but never touches the kinetic energy.
bronowski.9: A 1200 kg car rounds a flat circular bend of radius 45 m at a constant speed of 15 m s⁻¹. Determine the magnitude of the friction force the road must provide, and the minimum coefficient of friction that makes this possible. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
bronowski.10: Formula: the friction is the only horizontal force, so it alone must supply the centripetal force, Fnet = mv²r.
bronowski.12: Answer: Fnet = 6.0 × 10³ N, directed horizontally toward the centre of the bend.
bronowski.13: For the road to furnish this, the friction available, μmg, must reach at least 6.0 × 10³ N. On a flat road the normal force is N = mg = 1200 × 9.8 = 1.18 × 10⁴ N, so the least coefficient that will serve is μ = Fnet/N = 6.0 × 10³ ÷ 1.18 × 10⁴ = 0.51. Should the friction truly available fall short of this, the car cannot hold the bend, and it slides outward along a tangent.
bronowski.14: A road or track is banked when its surface is tilted toward the centre of the bend. By banking, we let a vehicle round the bend without leaning on friction at all. On a frictionless banked surface only two forces act upon the vehicle: its weight mg, drawn straight down, and the normal force N, drawn perpendicular to the tilted surface. And because the surface is tilted by an angle θ from the horizontal, the normal force is itself tilted by θ from the vertical.
bronowski.15: Let us resolve the normal force into its horizontal and vertical parts. The horizontal component, N sinθ, points toward the centre of the bend and supplies the centripetal force. The vertical component, N cosθ, points upward and balances the weight, for there is no vertical acceleration. Setting down both conditions:
bronowski.16: Divide the first equation by the second, and both N and m vanish, since sinθ/cosθ = tanθ:
bronowski.17: Rearranging delivers the centripetal acceleration and, with it, the design speed — that one speed at which a vehicle rounds the frictionless bank perfectly:
bronowski.18: At the design speed the outcome rests on r, g and θ alone — never on the mass of the vehicle. A truck and a motorcycle negotiate the same frictionless bank at the very same design speed.
bronowski.19: Key idea On a frictionless banked track the horizontal component of the normal force, N sinθ, supplies the centripetal force unaided, while the vertical component N cosθ balances the weight. From these two facts, and nothing more, follow ac = g tanθ and the design speed v = √(rg tanθ).
bronowski.20: Trap The normal force on a slope is not equal to mg. On a bank it is N = mg/cosθ, and this is larger than mg. Resolve N into its horizontal and vertical parts and write a separate equation for each direction; do not suppose the normal force balances the weight outright. At the design speed there is no friction — only the components of N act. Above the design speed the vehicle tends to slide up the bank, and friction points down the slope; below it the vehicle tends to slide down, and friction points up the slope.
bronowski.21: A test track is banked at 20° to the horizontal. A bend on the track has radius 80 m. Determine the design speed at which a vehicle rounds this bend with no reliance on friction, and the centripetal acceleration at that speed. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
bronowski.22: Formula: at the design speed the horizontal component of the normal force alone supplies the centripetal force, which gives v = √(rg tanθ) and ac = g tanθ.
bronowski.23: Substitution: ac = 9.8 × tan20° = 9.8 × 0.364 = 3.57 m s⁻², and v = √(80 × 9.8 × tan20°) = √(80 × 9.8 × 0.364) = √285
bronowski.24: Answer: v = 16.9 m s⁻¹ ≈ 17 m s⁻¹, with a centripetal acceleration of 3.6 m s⁻² directed horizontally toward the centre of the bend. The mass of the vehicle does not enter the calculation at all.
bronowski.25: When an object moves in a vertical circle — a ball on a string, a bucket of water swung overhead, a vehicle cresting a hump or dipping through a valley — gravity acts the same way throughout, always straight down; but its relationship to the centre of the circle shifts from point to point. We need the analysis only at the two extreme points, the highest point and the lowest point, where the net force, the weight and the tension (or normal force) all lie along the vertical line drawn through the centre.
bronowski.26: At the top of the circle the centre lies directly below the object. Both the weight mg and the string tension T point downward — toward the centre — and so they add. Newton’s second law, taken toward the centre, gives:
bronowski.27: At the bottom of the circle the centre lies directly above the object. Now the tension points upward toward the centre while the weight points downward away from it, and so they subtract. Taking toward the centre (upward) as positive:
bronowski.28: The tension at the bottom is therefore greater than at the top for the same speed, because at the bottom the tension must both bear up the weight and supply the centripetal force.
bronowski.29: As the object slows near the top, less tension is asked of the string. The minimum speed at the top is the speed at which the string just falls slack, so the tension drops to zero and gravity alone supplies the centripetal force. Setting T = 0 in the top equation:
bronowski.30: Below this speed the object can no longer hold the circular path at the top, and it falls inward. For a vehicle the supporting surface provides a normal force N in the place of tension. Cresting a hump (a convex crest), the centre lies below the vehicle, so mg − N = mv²/r, giving N = m(g − v²/r): the road pushes up with less than the weight, and the driver feels lighter. In a dip (a concave valley) the centre lies above, so N − mg = mv²/r, giving N = m(g + v²/r): the road pushes up harder than the weight, and the driver feels heavier. The normal force falls to zero, and the vehicle leaves the road, when v² = gr over the crest.
bronowski.31: Key idea At the top of a vertical circle both T and mg point toward the centre, and so they add: T + mg = mv²/r. At the bottom they oppose one another: T − mg = mv²/r. The string just falls slack at the top when v = √(gr).
bronowski.32: Trap The sign of the weight switches between the two points because the centre lies in a different direction, not because gravity itself has changed. Gravity is forever mg, straight down. At the top “down” is toward the centre, and so the weight adds to the tension; at the bottom “down” is away from the centre, and so the weight subtracts. Always settle which way the centre lies before you write Fnet = mv²/r, and never suppose the minimum-speed condition holds anywhere but the top.
bronowski.33: A 0.20 kg ball on the end of a string of length 0.80 m is swung in a vertical circle. Determine the minimum speed the ball can have at the highest point while the string stays taut, and the string tension at the highest point when the ball moves there at 4.0 m s⁻¹. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
bronowski.34: Formula (minimum speed): at the top the string is just slack, so T = 0 and mg = mv²/r, giving vmin = √(gr).
bronowski.36: Answer: vmin = 2.8 m s⁻¹. Below this speed the string falls slack and the ball falls inward.
bronowski.37: Formula (tension at 4.0 m s⁻¹): at the top both forces point toward the centre, so T + mg = mv²/r, giving T = mv²r − mg.
bronowski.39: Answer: T = 2.0 N, directed along the string toward the centre. Because 4.0 m s⁻¹ exceeds the minimum speed of 2.8 m s⁻¹, the tension is positive and the string stays taut.
bronowski.40: A satellite — whether a natural one such as the Moon or one made by human hands such as the International Space Station — can be modelled as an object in uniform circular motion. It travels around the central body at constant speed on a path of fixed radius, and so everything we have built up for circular motion holds: the velocity runs tangential, and the acceleration of magnitude v²/r points toward the centre of the orbit. The single force that supplies this centripetal acceleration is gravity; but the quantitative link between that force and the orbit — Newton’s law of universal gravitation, and the relation between orbital radius and period — belongs to Area of Study 2, and is developed in the Fields chapter. Here we treat the satellite purely as a case of circular motion.
bronowski.41: The radius that enters Fnet = mv²/r and a = v²/r is the orbital radius measured from the centre of the central body, in metres:
bronowski.42: The orbit is independent of the satellite’s mass. Both a = v²/r and the gravitational acceleration depend only upon the orbital radius and the central mass, and so two satellites of different mass that share the same orbit have the same speed, the same period, the same acceleration. A spanner let go by an astronaut keeps pace with the station beside it.

Second, the question “why is a satellite in a circular orbit accelerating?” is answered by Newton’s second law — an unbalanced (net) force causes acceleration toward the centre — never by the first or the third.

The full orbit derivation, geostationary satellites and apparent weightlessness are developed there — see Satellites in circular orbit in the Fields chapter.

bronowski.48: Formula: r = RE + altitude, then v = 2πrT and a = v²r.
bronowski.49: Substitution (radius): r = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 4.00 × 10⁵ = 6.77 × 10⁶ m, with the altitude converted from 400 km to 4.00 × 10⁵ m. The period is T = 92 × 60 = 5.52 × 10³ s.
bronowski.51: Answer: a = v²/r = (7.71 × 10³)² ÷ (6.77 × 10⁶) = 8.8 m s⁻², directed toward Earth’s centre. This equals the gravitational field strength at that altitude — which is the very reason gravity alone sustains the orbit — and the result does not depend on the station’s mass.

noether.3: The acceleration is radial — perpendicular to the velocity at every instant. By Newton’s second law a mass cannot accelerate unless an unbalanced force acts on it, so a net force must point toward the centre. This net force is the centripetal force:
noether.4: “Centripetal force” is not a new kind of force, and it never appears in a free-body diagram as a separate arrow. It is only the name given to the resultant of the real forces — gravity, tension, friction, the normal force — once they are added together. In every problem the first task is the same: identify which real force, or which component of a real force, points toward the centre and therefore supplies mv²/r.
noether.5: For a vehicle driving around a flat circular road, that force is friction between the tyres and the road, directed horizontally toward the centre of the bend. For an object whirled on a horizontal string, the force is the horizontal component of the tension in the string. In each case the net force, the acceleration and the radius are aligned along a single line, all pointing toward the centre.
noether.6: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity is tangential, while the acceleration and the net force both point toward the centre and have magnitude v²/r and mv²/r. What is preserved is the speed; what changes is the velocity.
noether.7: Trap There is no outward “centrifugal force” in this physics. Nothing pushes the object away from the centre. The outward feeling a passenger reports is the seat or door pushing them inward to keep them on the circle, together with their own inertia carrying them straight on. Never draw an outward force arrow, and never set the centripetal force equal to an imagined outward force.
noether.8: Connects The centripetal force is always perpendicular to the velocity, so it does zero work (work = force × displacement along the force). That is precisely why the speed in uniform circular motion is the conserved quantity — the net force turns the direction of motion but leaves the kinetic energy untouched.
noether.9: A 1200 kg car rounds a flat circular bend of radius 45 m at a constant speed of 15 m s⁻¹. Determine the magnitude of the friction force the road must provide, and the minimum coefficient of friction that makes this possible. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
noether.10: Formula: the friction force is the only horizontal force, so it alone supplies the centripetal force, Fnet = mv²r.
noether.12: Answer: Fnet = 6.0 × 10³ N, directed horizontally toward the centre of the bend.
noether.13: For the road to supply this, the available friction μmg must be at least 6.0 × 10³ N. On a flat road the normal force is N = mg = 1200 × 9.8 = 1.18 × 10⁴ N, so the minimum coefficient is μ = Fnet/N = 6.0 × 10³ ÷ 1.18 × 10⁴ = 0.51. If the friction actually available falls below this, the car can no longer hold the bend and slides outward along a tangent.
noether.14: A road or track is banked when its surface is tilted toward the centre of the bend. Banking allows a vehicle to round the bend without calling on friction at all. On a frictionless banked surface only two forces act on the vehicle: its weight mg, directed straight down, and the normal force N, directed perpendicular to the tilted surface. Because the surface is tilted by angle θ to the horizontal, the normal force is tilted by the same angle θ from the vertical.
noether.15: Resolve the normal force into horizontal and vertical components. The horizontal component, N sinθ, points toward the centre of the bend and supplies the centripetal force. The vertical component, N cosθ, points upward and balances the weight, because in the vertical direction nothing accelerates. Writing both conditions:
noether.16: Dividing the first equation by the second eliminates N and m at once, since sinθ/cosθ = tanθ:
noether.17: Rearranging gives the centripetal acceleration and the design speed — the one speed at which a vehicle rounds the frictionless bank in perfect balance:
noether.18: At the design speed the result depends only on r, g and θ — the mass of the vehicle has fallen out entirely. A truck and a motorcycle negotiate the same frictionless bank at the same design speed.
noether.19: Key idea On a frictionless banked track the horizontal component of the normal force, N sinθ, alone supplies the centripetal force, while the vertical component N cosθ balances the weight. This gives ac = g tanθ and the design speed v = √(rg tanθ).
noether.20: Trap The normal force on a slope is not equal to mg. On a bank it is N = mg/cosθ, which is larger than mg. Resolve N into horizontal and vertical components and write a separate equation for each direction; do not assume the normal force balances the weight directly. At the design speed there is no friction — only the components of N act. Above the design speed the balance is broken upward: the vehicle tends to slide up the bank and friction points down the slope; below it the balance is broken downward, the vehicle tends to slide down and friction points up the slope.
noether.21: A test track is banked at 20° to the horizontal. A bend on the track has radius 80 m. Determine the design speed at which a vehicle rounds this bend with no reliance on friction, and the centripetal acceleration at that speed. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
noether.22: Formula: at the design speed the horizontal component of the normal force alone supplies the centripetal force, giving v = √(rg tanθ) and ac = g tanθ.

noether.25: When an object moves in a vertical circle — a ball on a string, a bucket of water swung overhead, a vehicle crossing a hump or dipping through a valley — gravity is the one thing held fixed throughout, always mg straight down; what changes from point to point is its relationship to the centre of the circle. The analysis is required only at the two extreme points, the highest point and the lowest point, where the net force, the weight and the tension (or normal force) all lie along the single vertical line through the centre.
noether.26: At the top of the circle the centre is directly below the object. Both the weight mg and the string tension T point downward — toward the centre — so they add. Newton’s second law toward the centre gives:
noether.27: At the bottom of the circle the centre is directly above the object. The tension points upward toward the centre while the weight points downward away from it, so they subtract. Taking toward the centre (upward) as positive:
noether.28: The tension at the bottom is therefore greater than at the top for the same speed, because at the bottom the same tension must do two things at once — support the weight and supply the centripetal force.
noether.29: As the object slows near the top, less tension is needed. The minimum speed at the top is the speed at which the string just goes slack, where the tension falls to zero and gravity alone supplies the centripetal force. Setting T = 0 in the top equation:
noether.30: Below this speed the object can no longer hold the circular path at the top and falls inward. For a vehicle the supporting surface provides a normal force N in place of tension. Crossing a hump (a convex crest), the centre is below the vehicle, so mg − N = mv²/r, giving N = m(g − v²/r): the road pushes up less than the weight, and the driver feels lighter. noether.33: A 0.20 kg ball on the end of a string of length 0.80 m is swung in a vertical circle. Determine the minimum speed the ball can have at the highest point while the string stays taut, and the string tension at the highest point when the ball moves there at 4.0 m s⁻¹. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².

noether.36: Answer: vmin = 2.8 m s⁻¹. Below this speed the string goes slack and the ball falls inward.

noether.39: Answer: T = 2.0 N, directed along the string toward the centre. Because 4.0 m s⁻¹ exceeds the minimum speed of 2.8 m s⁻¹, the tension stays positive and the string stays taut.
noether.40: A satellite — whether a natural one such as the Moon or an artificial one such as the International Space Station — can be modelled as an object in uniform circular motion. Here the satellite is treated purely as a case of circular motion.

noether.42: The orbit is independent of the satellite’s mass. noether.47: The International Space Station orbits at an altitude of 400 km with an orbital period of 92 min. Modelling its motion as uniform circular motion, determine the orbital radius, the orbital speed and the centripetal acceleration. Take RE = 6.37 × 10⁶ m.
noether.48: Formula: r = RE + altitude, then v = 2πrT and a = v²r.
noether.49: Substitution (radius): r = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 4.00 × 10⁵ = 6.77 × 10⁶ m, with the altitude converted from 400 km to 4.00 × 10⁵ m. The period is T = 92 × 60 = 5.52 × 10³ s.

levin.15: Resolve the normal force into horizontal and vertical components. The horizontal component, N sinθ, points toward the centre of the bend and supplies the centripetal force. The vertical component, N cosθ, points upward and balances the weight, since there is no vertical acceleration. Writing both conditions:
levin.16: Divide the first equation by the second, and N and m fall away, since sinθ/cosθ = tanθ:
levin.17: Rearranging gives the centripetal acceleration and the design speed — the one speed at which a vehicle rounds the frictionless bank perfectly:
levin.18: At the design speed the result rests only on r, g and θ — never on the mass of the vehicle. A truck and a motorcycle take the same frictionless bank at the same design speed.
levin.19: Key idea On a frictionless banked track the horizontal component of the normal force, N sinθ, supplies the centripetal force alone, while the vertical component N cosθ balances the weight. This yields ac = g tanθ and the design speed v = √(rg tanθ).
levin.20: Trap The normal force on a slope is not equal to mg. On a bank it is N = mg/cosθ, larger than mg. Resolve N into horizontal and vertical components and write a separate equation for each direction; do not assume the normal force balances the weight outright. At the design speed there is no friction — only the components of N act. Above the design speed the vehicle tends to slide up the bank and friction points down the slope; below it the vehicle tends to slide down and friction points up the slope.
levin.21: A test track is banked at 20° to the horizontal. A bend on the track has radius 80 m. Determine the design speed at which a vehicle rounds this bend with no reliance on friction, and the centripetal acceleration at that speed. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
levin.22: Formula: at the design speed the horizontal component of the normal force supplies the centripetal force alone, giving v = √(rg tanθ) and ac = g tanθ.
levin.23: Substitution: ac = 9.8 × tan20° = 9.8 × 0.364 = 3.57 m s⁻², and v = √(80 × 9.8 × tan20°) = √(80 × 9.8 × 0.364) = √285
levin.24: Answer: v = 16.9 m s⁻¹ ≈ 17 m s⁻¹, with a centripetal acceleration of 3.6 m s⁻² directed horizontally toward the centre of the bend. The mass of the vehicle never enters the calculation.
levin.25: When an object moves in a vertical circle — a ball on a string, a bucket of water swung overhead, a vehicle cresting a hump or dipping through a valley — gravity acts the same throughout, straight down, yet its relationship to the centre of the circle shifts from point to point. The analysis is needed only at the two extremes, the highest point and the lowest point, where the net force, the weight and the tension (or normal force) all lie along the vertical line through the centre.
levin.26: At the top of the circle the centre lies directly below the object. Both the weight mg and the string tension T point downward — toward the centre — so they add. Newton’s second law toward the centre gives:
levin.27: At the bottom of the circle the centre lies directly above the object. The tension points upward toward the centre while the weight points downward away from it, so they subtract. Taking toward the centre (upward) as positive:
levin.28: The tension at the bottom is therefore greater than at the top for the same speed, because at the bottom the tension must both hold up the weight and supply the centripetal force.
levin.29: As the object slows near the top, less tension is asked of the string. The minimum speed at the top is the speed at which the string just goes slack, so the tension falls to zero and gravity alone supplies the centripetal force. Set T = 0 in the top equation:
levin.30: Below this speed the object cannot hold the circular path at the top and falls inward. For a vehicle the supporting surface gives a normal force N in place of tension. Cresting a hump (a convex crest), the centre lies below the vehicle, so mg − N = mv²/r, giving N = m(g − v²/r): the road pushes up less than the weight, and the driver feels lighter. In a dip (a concave valley) the centre lies above, so N − mg = mv²/r, giving N = m(g + v²/r): the road pushes up harder than the weight, and the driver feels heavier. The normal force falls to zero, and the vehicle leaves the road, when v² = gr over the crest.
levin.31: Key idea At the top of a vertical circle both T and mg point toward the centre, so T + mg = mv²/r. At the bottom they oppose, so T − mg = mv²/r. The string just goes slack at the top when v = √(gr).
levin.32: Trap The sign of the weight switches between the two points because the centre lies in a different direction, not because gravity changes. Gravity is always mg straight down. At the top “down” is toward the centre, so the weight adds to the tension; at the bottom “down” is away from the centre, so the weight subtracts. Always decide which way the centre lies before writing Fnet = mv²/r, and never assume the minimum-speed condition holds anywhere but the top.
levin.33: A 0.20 kg ball on the end of a string of length 0.80 m is swung in a vertical circle. Determine the minimum speed the ball can have at the highest point while the string stays taut, and the string tension at the highest point when the ball moves there at 4.0 m s⁻¹. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
levin.34: Formula (minimum speed): at the top the string is just slack, so T = 0 and mg = mv²/r, giving vmin = √(gr).
levin.36: Answer: vmin = 2.8 m s⁻¹. Below this speed the string goes slack and the ball falls inward.
levin.37: Formula (tension at 4.0 m s⁻¹): at the top both forces point toward the centre, so T + mg = mv²/r, giving T = mv²r − mg.
levin.39: Answer: T = 2.0 N, directed along the string toward the centre. Because 4.0 m s⁻¹ runs past the minimum speed of 2.8 m s⁻¹, the tension stays positive and the string holds taut.
levin.40: A satellite — a natural one such as the Moon, or an artificial one such as the International Space Station — can be modelled as an object in uniform circular motion. It rounds the central body at constant speed on a path of fixed radius, so everything settled for circular motion holds: the velocity runs tangential, and the acceleration of magnitude v²/r points toward the centre of the orbit. The single force that supplies this centripetal acceleration is gravity — but the quantitative link between that force and the orbit, Newton’s law of universal gravitation and the relationship between orbital radius and period, belongs to Area of Study 2 and is developed in the Fields chapter. Here the satellite is treated purely as a case of circular motion.
levin.41: The radius used in Fnet = mv²/r and a = v²/r is the orbital radius measured from the centre of the central body, in metres:
levin.42: The orbit is independent of the satellite’s mass. Both a = v²/r and the gravitational acceleration rest only on the orbital radius and the central mass, so two satellites of different mass sharing one orbit share the same speed, period and acceleration. A spanner released by an astronaut keeps pace with the station beside it.
levin.43: A satellite in a circular orbit is accelerating, even though its speed is constant, because its direction of motion is forever changing. The acceleration is explained by Newton’s second law: an unbalanced force — gravity — acts toward the centre, and an unbalanced force produces an acceleration along its own line. It is not explained by the first law (which describes motion when forces are balanced) or the third law (which concerns the equal and opposite force the satellite exerts on Earth).
levin.44: Key idea Model a satellite as uniform circular motion of radius r = RE + altitude (in metres). The orbit is independent of the satellite’s mass. The satellite accelerates because an unbalanced force — gravity — acts toward the centre: this is Newton’s second law.
levin.45: Trap Two recurring mark-losers. First, the orbital radius is not the altitude: an altitude of 400 km gives r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 4.00 × 10⁵ = 6.77 × 10⁶ m, not 4.00 × 10⁵ m. Always add RE and convert the altitude from km to m. Second, “why is a satellite in a circular orbit accelerating?” is answered by Newton’s second law — an unbalanced (net) force causes acceleration toward the centre — never the first or third law.
levin.46: Connects The reason gravity supplies exactly mv²/r, and how the orbital radius fixes the period, comes from Newton’s law of universal gravitation in Area of Study 2. The full orbit derivation, geostationary satellites and apparent weightlessness are developed there — see Satellites in circular orbit in the Fields chapter.
levin.47: The International Space Station orbits at an altitude of 400 km with an orbital period of 92 min. Modelling its motion as uniform circular motion, determine the orbital radius, the orbital speed and the centripetal acceleration. Take RE = 6.37 × 10⁶ m.
levin.48: Formula: r = RE + altitude, then v = 2πrT and a = v²r.
levin.49: Substitution (radius): r = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 4.00 × 10⁵ = 6.77 × 10⁶ m, with the altitude converted from 400 km to 4.00 × 10⁵ m. The period is T = 92 × 60 = 5.52 × 10³ s.
levin.51: Answer: a = v²/r = (7.71 × 10³)² ÷ (6.77 × 10⁶) = 8.8 m s⁻², directed toward Earth’s centre. This equals the gravitational field strength at that altitude — which is why gravity alone sustains the orbit — and the result does not depend on the station’s mass.

czerski.0: Swing a conker round on a string at a perfectly steady speed and, although nothing about how fast it’s going ever changes, the conker is accelerating at every single instant — simply because the way it’s pointing keeps changing. That’s the whole of this topic in one breath. Everything else reduces to a single detective job: work out which real force, aimed at the centre, is doing the pulling.
czerski.1: An object is in uniform circular motion when it loops round a circle of fixed radius at a steady speed. The speed holds constant — but the velocity does not, and that distinction is the heart of the matter. Velocity is a vector, and its direction is forever swinging round. At every point along the loop the velocity is tangential — pointing straight along the circle, at right angles to the radius, the way a stone flies off a sling. And because that direction never stops changing, the object is accelerating, even while its speed sits stubbornly the same.
czerski.2: The acceleration points inward, toward the centre of the circle — always tugging the object back from the straight line it would otherwise fly off along. We call it the centripetal acceleration (the word means “centre-seeking”), and for an object travelling at speed v round a circle of radius r its size is:
czerski.3: The acceleration is radial — pointing dead at the centre, at right angles to the velocity at every instant. Now, Newton’s second law is blunt about this: a mass simply cannot accelerate unless something unbalanced is shoving it, so there must be a net force aimed at the centre. That net force has a name — the centripetal force:
czerski.4: Here’s the thing that trips everyone up: “centripetal force” is not some new ingredient in the universe, and it never turns up in a free-body diagram as an arrow of its own. It’s simply the label we hang on the resultant of the real forces — gravity, tension, friction, the normal force — once you’ve added them all up. So in every single problem the first job is to hunt down which real force, or which component of a real force, points toward the centre and therefore does the work of supplying mv²/r.
czerski.5: Take a car cornering on a flat road: the force doing the pulling is friction between the tyres and the tarmac, aimed sideways toward the inside of the bend. Now whirl a ball on a horizontal string: this time it’s the horizontal component of the tension in the string. In every case the net force, the acceleration and the radius all line up along the same arrow, pointing inward.
czerski.6: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity points tangentially, while the acceleration and the net force both aim at the centre, with sizes v²/r and mv²/r. The speed stays constant; the velocity never does.
czerski.7: Trap There is no outward “centrifugal force” in this physics — full stop. Nothing is flinging the object away from the centre. That outward shove a passenger swears they can feel is really the seat or the door pressing them inward, dragging them round the bend, while their own inertia tries to carry them straight on. Never draw an outward arrow, and never set the centripetal force equal to some imagined outward push.
czerski.8: Connects The centripetal force always sits at right angles to the velocity, which means it does zero work (work = force × displacement along the force). And that is exactly why the speed in uniform circular motion never budges — the net force keeps swinging the direction of motion round and round, but never adds a scrap of kinetic energy.
czerski.9: A 1200 kg car rounds a flat circular bend of radius 45 m at a constant speed of 15 m s⁻¹. Determine the magnitude of the friction force the road must provide, and the minimum coefficient of friction that makes this possible. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
czerski.10: Formula: friction is the only horizontal force in play, so it’s the one supplying the centripetal force, Fnet = mv²r.
czerski.12: Answer: Fnet = 6.0 × 10³ N, pointing horizontally toward the centre of the bend.
czerski.13: For the road to deliver this, the friction it has on offer, μmg, must be at least 6.0 × 10³ N. On a flat road the normal force is simply N = mg = 1200 × 9.8 = 1.18 × 10⁴ N, so the smallest coefficient that will do the job is μ = Fnet/N = 6.0 × 10³ ÷ 1.18 × 10⁴ = 0.51. If the road has less grip than that on offer, the car simply can’t hold the bend and slides outward along a tangent, straight off the line it was trying to follow.
czerski.14: A road or track is banked when its surface is tilted inward, leaning down toward the centre of the bend — think of the steep curve on a cycling velodrome. Banking lets a vehicle round the corner without leaning on friction at all. On a perfectly frictionless banked surface just two forces touch the vehicle: its weight mg, pulling straight down, and the normal force N, pushing at right angles to the tilted surface. Because the surface is tilted by angle θ from the horizontal, that normal force ends up tilted by the very same θ from the vertical.
czerski.15: Now split the normal force into its horizontal and vertical parts. The horizontal component, N sinθ, leans toward the centre of the bend — and this is the part doing the centripetal work. The vertical component, N cosθ, points up and exactly props up the weight, since the vehicle isn’t accelerating up or down. Writing both conditions down:
czerski.16: Divide the first equation by the second and something neat happens — both N and m vanish, since sinθ/cosθ = tanθ:
czerski.17: Rearranging hands us the centripetal acceleration and the design speed — the one magic speed at which a vehicle sails round the frictionless bank perfectly, without sliding up or slipping down:
czerski.18: At that design speed the answer leans only on r, g and θ — never once on the mass of the vehicle. A loaded truck and a featherweight motorcycle take the same frictionless bank at exactly the same design speed.

The safe move is always to split N into horizontal and vertical parts and write a separate equation for each direction; never just assume the normal force balances the weight head-on. At the design speed there’s no friction at all — only the components of N are doing anything. Push above the design speed and the vehicle starts to creep up the bank, so friction points down the slope; drop below it and the vehicle tends to slide down, so friction points up the slope.
czerski.21: A test track is banked at 20° to the horizontal. A bend on the track has radius 80 m. Determine the design speed at which a vehicle rounds this bend with no reliance on friction, and the centripetal acceleration at that speed. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
