czerski.23: Substitution: ac = 9.8 × tan20° = 9.8 × 0.364 = 3.57 m s⁻², and v = √(80 × 9.8 × tan20°) = √(80 × 9.8 × 0.364) = √285
czerski.24: Answer: v = 16.9 m s⁻¹ ≈ 17 m s⁻¹, with a centripetal acceleration of 3.6 m s⁻² pointing horizontally toward the centre of the bend. Notice the mass of the vehicle never once enters the sum.
czerski.25: When an object travels round a vertical circle — a ball on a string, a bucket of water swung over your head, a car cresting a hump or plunging through a dip — gravity behaves exactly the same all the way round (straight down, as ever), but how it lines up with the centre of the circle keeps shifting from point to point. Happily, you only ever need to do the sums at the two extremes, the highest point and the lowest point, where the net force, the weight and the tension (or normal force) all fall neatly along the vertical line through the centre.
czerski.26: At the top of the circle the centre sits directly below the object. Both the weight mg and the string tension T point downward — both toward the centre — so they team up and add together. Newton’s second law toward the centre gives:
czerski.27: At the bottom of the circle the centre is now directly above the object. Here the tension hauls upward toward the centre while the weight pulls down, away from it, so the two work against each other and subtract. Taking toward the centre (upward) as positive:
czerski.28: So the tension at the bottom comes out greater than at the top for the very same speed — and that makes sense, because down at the bottom the tension has two jobs at once: holding up the weight and supplying the centripetal pull.
czerski.29: As the object slows down near the top, it needs less and less tension to stay on the circle. The minimum speed at the top is the speed at which the string just goes limp — the tension drops to zero and gravity alone is left to supply the whole centripetal force. Setting T = 0 in the top equation:
czerski.30: Go any slower than this and the object simply can’t hold the circle at the top — it falls inward. For a vehicle the road underneath provides a normal force N in place of the string’s tension. Crest a hump (a convex rise) and the centre is below the vehicle, so mg − N = mv²/r, giving N = m(g − v²/r): the road pushes up with less than the full weight, and your stomach lifts — you feel lighter. Drop through a dip (a concave valley) and the centre is above, so N − mg = mv²/r, giving N = m(g + v²/r): now the road shoves up harder than the weight, and you’re pressed into the seat, feeling heavier. The normal force falls to zero, and the vehicle actually leaves the road, when v² = gr over the crest.
czerski.31: Key idea At the top of a vertical circle both T and mg point toward the centre, so T + mg = mv²/r. At the bottom they pull against each other, so T − mg = mv²/r. The string just goes slack at the top when v = √(gr).
czerski.32: Trap The weight seems to flip sign between the two points — but it’s only because the centre lies in a different direction, not because gravity itself has changed one bit. Gravity is always mg straight down, the whole way round. At the top “down” happens to be toward the centre, so the weight adds to the tension; at the bottom “down” is away from the centre, so the weight subtracts. Always settle which way the centre lies before you write Fnet = mv²/r, and never assume the minimum-speed condition applies anywhere but the very top.
czerski.33: A 0.20 kg ball on the end of a string of length 0.80 m is swung in a vertical circle. Determine the minimum speed the ball can have at the highest point while the string stays taut, and the string tension at the highest point when the ball moves there at 4.0 m s⁻¹. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².

czerski.36: Answer: vmin = 2.8 m s⁻¹. Any slower and the string goes slack and the ball drops inward.
czerski.37: Formula (tension at 4.0 m s⁻¹): at the top both forces point toward the centre, so T + mg = mv²/r, giving T = mv²r − mg.
czerski.39: Answer: T = 2.0 N, pulling along the string toward the centre. And because 4.0 m s⁻¹ comfortably beats the minimum speed of 2.8 m s⁻¹, the tension comes out positive and the string stays taut.

The full orbit derivation, geostationary satellites and that strange business of apparent weightlessness are developed there — see Satellites in circular orbit in the Fields chapter.

So resolve N into horizontal and vertical components and write a separate equation for each direction; don't assume the normal force balances the weight on its own. Above the design speed the vehicle tends to slide up the bank and friction points down the slope; below it the vehicle tends to slide down and friction points up the slope.
veritasium.21: A test track is banked at 20° to the horizontal. A bend on the track has radius 80 m. Determine the design speed at which a vehicle rounds this bend with no reliance on friction, and the centripetal acceleration at that speed. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
veritasium.22: Formula: at the design speed the horizontal component of the normal force does the whole job of supplying the centripetal force, which gives v = √(rg tanθ) and ac = g tanθ.
veritasium.23: Substitution: ac = 9.8 × tan20° = 9.8 × 0.364 = 3.57 m s⁻², and v = √(80 × 9.8 × tan20°) = √(80 × 9.8 × 0.364) = √285
veritasium.24: Answer: v = 16.9 m s⁻¹ ≈ 17 m s⁻¹, with a centripetal acceleration of 3.6 m s⁻² directed horizontally toward the centre of the bend. Notice the mass of the vehicle never entered the calculation.
veritasium.25: Now move the circle into a vertical plane — a ball on a string, a bucket of water swung overhead, a vehicle cresting a hump or dropping through a valley. You might assume gravity does something different up top versus down below, but it doesn't: it pulls straight down everywhere, the same as always. What changes is its relationship to the centre of the circle, point by point. And you only ever need to analyse the two extremes, the highest point and the lowest point, where the net force, the weight and the tension (or normal force) all sit along the vertical line through the centre.
veritasium.26: At the top of the circle the centre is directly below the object. So both the weight mg and the string tension T point downward — both toward the centre — and they add together. Newton's second law toward the centre gives:
veritasium.27: At the bottom of the circle the centre is directly above the object. Now the tension points up toward the centre while the weight points down, away from it, so this time they subtract. Taking toward the centre (upward) as positive:
veritasium.28: So the tension at the bottom comes out greater than at the top for the same speed — and here's why: at the bottom the tension has to do two jobs at once, holding up the weight and supplying the centripetal force.
veritasium.29: As the object slows down near the top, it needs less and less tension. The minimum speed at the top is the speed at which the string just barely goes slack — the tension drops to zero and gravity alone has to supply the centripetal force. Set T = 0 in the top equation:
veritasium.30: Go any slower than that and the object simply can't hold the circular path at the top — it falls inward. For a vehicle the road provides a normal force N instead of tension. Cresting a hump (a convex crest), the centre is below the vehicle, so mg − N = mv²/r, giving N = m(g − v²/r): the road pushes up with less than the weight, and the driver feels lighter. In a dip (a concave valley) the centre is above, so N − mg = mv²/r, giving N = m(g + v²/r): the road pushes up harder than the weight, and the driver feels heavier. The normal force drops to zero — and the vehicle leaves the road — when v² = gr over the crest.
veritasium.31: Key idea At the top of a vertical circle both T and mg point toward the centre, so T + mg = mv²/r. At the bottom they oppose, so T − mg = mv²/r. The string just goes slack at the top when v = √(gr).
veritasium.32: Trap It's tempting to think gravity itself flips sign between the top and the bottom. It doesn't. Gravity is always mg straight down, full stop. What flips is which way the centre lies. At the top “down” is toward the centre, so the weight adds to the tension; at the bottom “down” points away from the centre, so the weight subtracts. So always work out which way the centre lies before you write Fnet = mv²/r, and never assume the minimum-speed condition holds anywhere except the top.
veritasium.33: A 0.20 kg ball on the end of a string of length 0.80 m is swung in a vertical circle. Determine the minimum speed the ball can have at the highest point while the string stays taut, and the string tension at the highest point when the ball moves there at 4.0 m s⁻¹. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
veritasium.34: Formula (minimum speed): at the top the string is just on the edge of going slack, so T = 0 and mg = mv²/r, which gives vmin = √(gr).
veritasium.36: Answer: vmin = 2.8 m s⁻¹. Below this speed the string goes slack and the ball falls inward.
veritasium.37: Formula (tension at 4.0 m s⁻¹): at the top both forces point toward the centre, so T + mg = mv²/r, which gives T = mv²r − mg.
veritasium.39: Answer: T = 2.0 N, directed along the string toward the centre. Because 4.0 m s⁻¹ comfortably beats the minimum speed of 2.8 m s⁻¹, the tension comes out positive and the string stays taut.
veritasium.40: A satellite — a natural one like the Moon, or an artificial one like the International Space Station — can be modelled as nothing more exotic than an object in uniform circular motion. It travels around the central body at constant speed on a path of fixed radius, so everything you've already worked out for circular motion carries straight over: the velocity is tangential, and the acceleration of magnitude v²/r points toward the centre of the orbit. The one force supplying that centripetal acceleration is gravity — but the quantitative link between that force and the orbit, Newton's law of universal gravitation and the relationship between orbital radius and period, belongs to Area of Study 2 and is built up in the Fields chapter. Here the satellite is treated purely as a case of circular motion.
veritasium.41: One thing to get right: the radius in Fnet = mv²/r and a = v²/r is the orbital radius measured from the centre of the central body, in metres:
veritasium.42: Here's the surprise: the orbit is completely independent of the satellite's mass. Both a = v²/r and the gravitational acceleration depend only on the orbital radius and the central mass, so two satellites of wildly different mass sharing the same orbit have the same speed, the same period, the same acceleration. Drop a spanner from the station and it doesn't fall behind — it keeps pace right alongside, in its own orbit.
veritasium.43: A satellite in a circular orbit is accelerating, even though its speed is constant, because its direction of motion is always changing. And the law that explains it is Newton's second law: an unbalanced force — gravity — acts toward the centre, and an unbalanced force produces an acceleration in its own direction. It is not the first law (which describes motion when forces are balanced) and not the third law (which is about the equal and opposite force the satellite exerts back on Earth).
veritasium.44: Key idea Model a satellite as uniform circular motion of radius r = RE + altitude (in metres). The orbit is independent of the satellite's mass. The satellite accelerates because an unbalanced force — gravity — acts toward the centre: this is Newton's second law.
veritasium.45: Trap Two mistakes cost marks again and again. First, the orbital radius is not the altitude — it's tempting to just plug in the height, but an altitude of 400 km gives r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 4.00 × 10⁵ = 6.77 × 10⁶ m, not 4.00 × 10⁵ m. Always add RE and convert the altitude from km to m. Second, “why is a satellite in a circular orbit accelerating?” is answered by Newton's second law — an unbalanced (net) force causes acceleration toward the centre — never the first or third law.
veritasium.46: Connects Why does gravity supply exactly mv²/r, and how does the orbital radius pin down the period? That comes from Newton's law of universal gravitation in Area of Study 2. The full orbit derivation, geostationary satellites and apparent weightlessness are all developed there — see Satellites in circular orbit in the Fields chapter.
veritasium.47: The International Space Station orbits at an altitude of 400 km with an orbital period of 92 min. Modelling its motion as uniform circular motion, determine the orbital radius, the orbital speed and the centripetal acceleration. Take RE = 6.37 × 10⁶ m.
veritasium.48: Formula: r = RE + altitude, then v = 2πrT and a = v²r.
veritasium.49: Substitution (radius): r = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 4.00 × 10⁵ = 6.77 × 10⁶ m, with the altitude converted from 400 km to 4.00 × 10⁵ m. The period is T = 92 × 60 = 5.52 × 10³ s.
veritasium.51: Answer: a = v²/r = (7.71 × 10³)² ÷ (6.77 × 10⁶) = 8.8 m s⁻², directed toward Earth's centre. And here's the punchline: this equals the gravitational field strength at that altitude, which is exactly why gravity alone is enough to sustain the orbit — and why the result doesn't depend on the station's mass at all.

attenborough.0: Watch closely. An object gliding at constant speed around a circle is, at every instant, accelerating — for its direction of travel is forever turning. The whole of this topic comes down to a single quiet task: to find which real force, pointing inward toward the centre, supplies that ceaseless acceleration.
attenborough.1: An object moves in uniform circular motion when it travels around a circle of fixed radius at constant speed. The speed holds steady — but the velocity does not. Velocity is a vector, and its direction shifts continuously, moment by moment. At every point along the path the velocity is tangential, lying along the circle, perpendicular to the radius. And because that direction is always changing, the object is accelerating — even though its speed never wavers at all.
attenborough.2: The acceleration points inward, toward the very centre of the circle. We call it the centripetal acceleration — “centre-seeking” — and for an object of speed v travelling on a circle of radius r, its magnitude is this:
attenborough.3: The acceleration is radial, perpendicular to the velocity at every instant. And by Newton’s second law a mass cannot accelerate unless some unbalanced force acts upon it — so a net force must point toward the centre. This net force we name the centripetal force:
attenborough.4: Now, “centripetal force” is no new kind of force, and it never appears in a free-body diagram as an arrow of its own. It is simply the name we give to the resultant of the real forces — gravity, tension, friction, the normal force — once they have all been gathered and added together. In every problem the first task is the same: to identify which real force, or which component of a real force, points toward the centre and so supplies mv²/r.
attenborough.5: For a vehicle sweeping around a flat circular road, that force is friction, between the tyres and the road, directed horizontally toward the centre of the bend. For an object whirled on the end of a horizontal string, it is the horizontal component of the tension in the string. In each case the net force, the acceleration and the radius all lie along the same line — pointing inward, toward the centre.
attenborough.6: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity is tangential, while the acceleration and the net force both point toward the centre, with magnitudes v²/r and mv²/r. The speed is constant; the velocity is not.
attenborough.7: Trap Beware: there is no outward “centrifugal force” in this physics. Nothing pushes the object away from the centre. That outward feeling a passenger reports is the seat or the door pressing them inward, holding them to the circle — together with their own inertia, which would carry them straight on into the night. Never draw an outward arrow, and never set the centripetal force equal to some imagined outward force.
attenborough.8: Connects The centripetal force is always perpendicular to the velocity, and so it does zero work (work = force × displacement along the force). That, precisely, is why the speed in uniform circular motion holds constant — the net force turns the direction of motion, yet never touches the kinetic energy.
attenborough.9: A 1200 kg car rounds a flat circular bend of radius 45 m at a constant speed of 15 m s⁻¹. Determine the magnitude of the friction force the road must provide, and the minimum coefficient of friction that makes this possible. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
attenborough.10: Formula: the friction force is the only horizontal force, so it alone supplies the centripetal force, Fnet = mv²r.
attenborough.12: Answer: Fnet = 6.0 × 10³ N, directed horizontally toward the centre of the bend.
attenborough.13: For the road to provide this, the available friction μmg must be at least 6.0 × 10³ N. On a flat road the normal force is N = mg = 1200 × 9.8 = 1.18 × 10⁴ N, and so the minimum coefficient is μ = Fnet/N = 6.0 × 10³ ÷ 1.18 × 10⁴ = 0.51. Should the friction actually available fall short of this, the car cannot hold the bend, and it slides outward along a tangent — away into the open.
attenborough.14: A road or track is banked when its surface is tilted toward the centre of the bend. This tilt allows a vehicle to round the bend without leaning on friction at all. On a frictionless banked surface only two forces act upon the vehicle: its weight mg, drawn straight down, and the normal force N, directed perpendicular to the tilted surface. And because the surface is tilted by an angle θ to the horizontal, the normal force too is tilted — by that same θ from the vertical.
attenborough.15: Let us resolve the normal force into its horizontal and vertical components. The horizontal component, N sinθ, points toward the centre of the bend and supplies the centripetal force. The vertical component, N cosθ, points upward and balances the weight, since there is no vertical acceleration. We write both conditions:
attenborough.16: Dividing the first equation by the second sweeps away both N and m, for sinθ/cosθ = tanθ:
attenborough.17: Rearranging gives, at last, the centripetal acceleration and the design speed — that single speed at which a vehicle rounds the frictionless bank perfectly:
attenborough.18: At the design speed the result depends only upon r, g and θ — never upon the mass of the vehicle. A truck and a motorcycle negotiate the very same frictionless bank at one and the same design speed.
attenborough.19: Key idea On a frictionless banked track the horizontal component of the normal force, N sinθ, alone supplies the centripetal force, while the vertical component N cosθ balances the weight. This gives ac = g tanθ and the design speed v = √(rg tanθ).
attenborough.20: Trap The normal force on a slope is not equal to mg. On a bank it is N = mg/cosθ, larger than mg. Resolve N into its horizontal and vertical components and write a separate equation for each direction; never assume the normal force balances the weight directly. At the design speed there is no friction — only the components of N are at work. Above the design speed the vehicle tends to drift up the bank, and friction points down the slope; below it the vehicle tends to slide down, and friction points up the slope.
attenborough.21: A test track is banked at 20° to the horizontal. A bend on the track has radius 80 m. Determine the design speed at which a vehicle rounds this bend with no reliance on friction, and the centripetal acceleration at that speed. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
attenborough.22: Formula: at the design speed the horizontal component of the normal force alone supplies the centripetal force, giving v = √(rg tanθ) and ac = g tanθ.
attenborough.23: Substitution: ac = 9.8 × tan20° = 9.8 × 0.364 = 3.57 m s⁻², and v = √(80 × 9.8 × tan20°) = √(80 × 9.8 × 0.364) = √285
attenborough.24: Answer: v = 16.9 m s⁻¹ ≈ 17 m s⁻¹, with a centripetal acceleration of 3.6 m s⁻² directed horizontally toward the centre of the bend. The mass of the vehicle never enters the calculation at all.
attenborough.25: When an object travels in a vertical circle — a ball on a string, a bucket of water swung overhead, a vehicle cresting a hump or dipping through a valley — gravity acts the same way throughout, always straight down; yet its relationship to the centre of the circle shifts from point to point. The analysis is needed only at the two extremes, the highest point and the lowest point, where the net force, the weight and the tension (or the normal force) all lie along the single vertical line through the centre.
attenborough.26: At the top of the circle the centre lies directly below the object. Here both the weight mg and the string tension T point downward — toward the centre — and so they add. Newton’s second law, taken toward the centre, gives:
attenborough.27: At the bottom of the circle the centre lies directly above. Now the tension points upward toward the centre while the weight points downward, away from it, and so they subtract. Taking toward the centre — upward — as positive:
attenborough.28: The tension at the bottom is therefore greater than at the top, for the same speed — because at the bottom the tension must do two things at once: support the weight, and supply the centripetal force.
attenborough.29: As the object slows near the top, less tension is called for. The minimum speed at the top is the speed at which the string just goes slack, the tension falling to zero, and gravity alone is left to supply the centripetal force. Setting T = 0 in the top equation:
attenborough.30: Below this speed the object can no longer hold the circular path at the top, and it falls inward. For a vehicle the supporting surface provides a normal force N in place of tension. Cresting a hump — a convex rise — the centre lies below the vehicle, so mg − N = mv²/r, giving N = m(g − v²/r): the road pushes up with less than the weight, and the driver feels lighter. In a dip — a concave valley — the centre lies above, so N − mg = mv²/r, giving N = m(g + v²/r): the road pushes up harder than the weight, and the driver feels heavier. The normal force falls to zero, and the vehicle leaves the road altogether, when v² = gr over the crest.
attenborough.31: Key idea At the top of a vertical circle both T and mg point toward the centre, so T + mg = mv²/r. At the bottom they oppose, so T − mg = mv²/r. The string just goes slack at the top when v = √(gr).
attenborough.32: Trap The sign of the weight switches between the two points because the centre lies in a different direction — not because gravity itself has changed. Gravity is always mg straight down. At the top, “down” is toward the centre, so the weight adds to the tension; at the bottom, “down” is away from the centre, so the weight subtracts. Always decide which way the centre lies before you write Fnet = mv²/r, and never assume the minimum-speed condition holds anywhere but the top.
attenborough.33: A 0.20 kg ball on the end of a string of length 0.80 m is swung in a vertical circle. Determine the minimum speed the ball can have at the highest point while the string stays taut, and the string tension at the highest point when the ball moves there at 4.0 m s⁻¹. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
attenborough.34: Formula (minimum speed): at the top the string is just slack, so T = 0 and mg = mv²/r, giving vmin = √(gr).
attenborough.36: Answer: vmin = 2.8 m s⁻¹. Below this speed the string goes slack and the ball falls inward.
attenborough.37: Formula (tension at 4.0 m s⁻¹): at the top both forces point toward the centre, so T + mg = mv²/r, giving T = mv²r − mg.
attenborough.39: Answer: T = 2.0 N, directed along the string toward the centre. And because 4.0 m s⁻¹ exceeds the minimum speed of 2.8 m s⁻¹, the tension is positive and the string stays taut.
attenborough.40: A satellite — whether a natural one, such as the Moon, or an artificial one, such as the International Space Station — may be modelled as an object in uniform circular motion. It circles the central body at constant speed, on a path of fixed radius, and so everything we have established for circular motion holds true here: the velocity is tangential, and the acceleration, of magnitude v²/r, points toward the centre of the orbit. The single force that supplies this centripetal acceleration is gravity — but the quantitative link between that force and the orbit, Newton’s law of universal gravitation and the relationship between orbital radius and period, belongs to Area of Study 2 and is developed in the Fields chapter. Here the satellite is treated purely as a case of circular motion.
attenborough.41: The radius used in Fnet = mv²/r and a = v²/r is the orbital radius measured from the centre of the central body, in metres:
attenborough.42: The orbit is independent of the satellite’s mass. Both a = v²/r and the gravitational acceleration depend only upon the orbital radius and the central mass, and so two satellites of different mass, sharing one orbit, share the same speed, the same period, the same acceleration. A spanner released by an astronaut keeps perfect pace with the station beside it, drifting on as though held by an invisible thread.

The acceleration is explained by Newton’s second law: an unbalanced force, gravity, acts toward the centre, and an unbalanced force produces an acceleration in its own direction. It is not explained by the first law, which describes motion when forces are balanced, nor by the third, which concerns the equal and opposite force the satellite exerts upon the Earth.

Always add RE, and always convert the altitude from km to m. Second, “why is a satellite in a circular orbit accelerating?” is answered by Newton’s second law — an unbalanced (net) force causes acceleration toward the centre — never by the first or the third law.
attenborough.46: Connects The reason gravity supplies exactly mv²/r, and the way the orbital radius fixes the period, comes from Newton’s law of universal gravitation in Area of Study 2. The full orbit derivation, geostationary satellites and apparent weightlessness are developed there — see Satellites in circular orbit in the Fields chapter.

attenborough.48: Formula: r = RE + altitude, then v = 2πrT and a = v²r.
attenborough.49: Substitution (radius): r = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 4.00 × 10⁵ = 6.77 × 10⁶ m, the altitude converted from 400 km to 4.00 × 10⁵ m. The period is T = 92 × 60 = 5.52 × 10³ s.
attenborough.51: Answer: a = v²/r = (7.71 × 10³)² ÷ (6.77 × 10⁶) = 8.8 m s⁻², directed toward the Earth’s centre. This equals the gravitational field strength at that altitude — which is why gravity alone is enough to sustain the orbit — and the result does not depend upon the station’s mass.

greekepic.29: As the body slows near the top, less tension is demanded. The minimum speed at the top is the speed at which the string just falls slack, so that the tension sinks to zero and gravity alone grants the centripetal force. Setting T = 0 in the top equation:
greekepic.30: Below this speed the body cannot hold the circular path at the top and falls inward. For a vehicle the bearing surface grants a normal force N in place of tension. Crossing a hump (a convex crest), the centre lies below the vehicle, so mg − N = mv²/r, giving N = m(g − v²/r): the road thrusts up less than the weight, and the driver feels the lighter. In a dip (a concave valley) the centre lies above, so N − mg = mv²/r, giving N = m(g + v²/r): the road thrusts up harder than the weight, and the driver feels the heavier. The normal force sinks to zero, and the vehicle forsakes the road, when v² = gr over the crest.
greekepic.31: Key idea At the top of a vertical circle both T and mg point toward the centre, so T + mg = mv²/r. At the bottom they stand opposed, so T − mg = mv²/r. The string just falls slack at the top when v = √(gr).
greekepic.32: Trap The sign of the weight turns between the two points because the centre lies in a different quarter, not because gravity itself alters. Gravity is forever mg straight down. At the top “down” is toward the centre, so the weight adds to the tension; at the bottom “down” is away from the centre, so the weight subtracts. Always decree which way the centre lies before writing Fnet = mv²/r, and never presume the minimum-speed condition holds anywhere save the top.
greekepic.33: A 0.20 kg ball on the end of a string of length 0.80 m is swung in a vertical circle. Determine the minimum speed the ball can have at the highest point while the string stays taut, and the string tension at the highest point when the ball moves there at 4.0 m s⁻¹. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
greekepic.34: Formula (minimum speed): at the top the string is just slack, so T = 0 and mg = mv²/r, giving vmin = √(gr).
greekepic.36: Answer: vmin = 2.8 m s⁻¹. Below this speed the string falls slack and the ball plunges inward.
greekepic.37: Formula (tension at 4.0 m s⁻¹): at the top both forces point toward the centre, so T + mg = mv²/r, giving T = mv²r − mg.
greekepic.39: Answer: T = 2.0 N, borne along the string toward the centre. Because 4.0 m s⁻¹ outstrips the minimum speed of 2.8 m s⁻¹, the tension stands positive and the string holds taut.
greekepic.40: A satellite — be it a natural one such as the Moon or a wrought one such as the International Space Station — may be modelled as a body in uniform circular motion. It travels about the central body at constant speed upon a path of fixed radius, and so all that was decreed for circular motion holds true: the velocity runs tangential, and the acceleration of magnitude v²/r points toward the centre of the orbit. The single force that grants this centripetal acceleration is gravity, yet the quantitative bond between that force and the orbit — Newton’s law of universal gravitation and the kinship of orbital radius and period — belongs to Area of Study 2 and is unfolded in the Fields chapter. Here the satellite is reckoned purely as a case of circular motion.
greekepic.41: The radius borne in Fnet = mv²/r and a = v²/r is the orbital radius measured from the centre of the central body, in metres:
greekepic.42: The orbit stands independent of the satellite’s mass. Both a = v²/r and the gravitational acceleration hang upon the orbital radius and the central mass alone, and so two satellites of differing mass sharing one orbit bear the same speed, period and acceleration. A spanner loosed by an astronaut keeps pace with the station beside it.
greekepic.43: A satellite in a circular orbit is accelerating, though its speed holds constant, for the heading of its journey is forever turning. The acceleration is unfolded by Newton’s second law: an unbalanced force — gravity — lays hold toward the centre, and an unbalanced force grants an acceleration in its own quarter. It is not unfolded by the first law (which proclaims motion when forces stand in balance) nor the third law (which concerns the equal and opposite force the satellite exerts upon Earth).
greekepic.44: Key idea Model a satellite as uniform circular motion of radius r = RE + altitude (in metres). The orbit stands independent of the satellite’s mass. The satellite accelerates because an unbalanced force — gravity — lays hold toward the centre: this is Newton’s second law.
greekepic.45: Trap Two recurring thieves of marks. First, the orbital radius is not the altitude: an altitude of 400 km gives r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 4.00 × 10⁵ = 6.77 × 10⁶ m, not 4.00 × 10⁵ m. Always add RE and turn the altitude from km into m. Second, “why is a satellite in a circular orbit accelerating?” is answered by Newton’s second law — an unbalanced (net) force grants acceleration toward the centre — never the first or third law.
greekepic.46: Connects The reason gravity grants exactly mv²/r, and how the orbital radius fixes the period, springs from Newton’s law of universal gravitation in Area of Study 2. The full orbit derivation, geostationary satellites and apparent weightlessness are unfolded there — see Satellites in circular orbit in the Fields chapter.
greekepic.47: The International Space Station orbits at an altitude of 400 km with an orbital period of 92 min. Modelling its motion as uniform circular motion, determine the orbital radius, the orbital speed and the centripetal acceleration. Take RE = 6.37 × 10⁶ m.
greekepic.48: Formula: r = RE + altitude, then v = 2πrT and a = v²r.
greekepic.49: Substitution (radius): r = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 4.00 × 10⁵ = 6.77 × 10⁶ m, with the altitude turned from 400 km into 4.00 × 10⁵ m. The period is T = 92 × 60 = 5.52 × 10³ s.
greekepic.51: Answer: a = v²/r = (7.71 × 10³)² ÷ (6.77 × 10⁶) = 8.8 m s⁻², borne toward Earth’s centre. This stands equal to the gravitational field strength at that altitude, which is why gravity alone sustains the orbit — and the result hangs not upon the station’s mass.

cnpop.0: 一个物体沿着圆周匀速转圈，看着速率稳如老狗，可它其实每一瞬间都在加速——因为它的运动方向一直在变。整节课说白了就一个任务：找出那个指向圆心的真实的力，到底是谁在给这份加速度买单。
cnpop.1: 当一个物体绕着固定半径的圆周、以恒定速率运动时，我们就说它在做匀速圆周运动。注意，速率是恒定的，可速度并不是：速度是个矢量，它的方向一直在变。在每一点上，速度都是切向的——沿着圆周走，跟半径垂直。正因为速度方向时刻在变，物体就一直在加速，哪怕它的速率从头到尾纹丝不动。
cnpop.2: 这个加速度指向圆心。我们管它叫向心加速度（顾名思义就是“朝圆心冲”），对一个速率为 v、在半径 r 的圆上跑的物体，它的大小就是：
cnpop.3: 加速度是径向的——每一瞬间都跟速度垂直。按牛顿第二定律，没有不平衡的力，质量是没法加速的，所以必须有一个净力指向圆心。这个净力就叫向心力：
cnpop.4: 划重点：“向心力”根本不是什么新种类的力，它永远不会作为一根单独的箭头出现在受力分析图里。它只是个名字，专门用来称呼那些真实的力——重力、张力、摩擦力、法向力——加起来之后的合力。每道题的第一步永远是搞清楚到底是哪个真实的力，或者哪个真实力的分量，正好指向圆心，从而扛起 mv²/r 这份活。
cnpop.5: 对一辆在平坦圆形路面上转弯的车来说，干这活的是轮胎和路面之间的摩擦力，水平指向弯道圆心。对一个被水平绳子甩着转的物体来说，干这活的是绳中张力的水平分量。两种情况里，净力、加速度和半径全都沿着同一条线，齐刷刷指向圆心。
cnpop.6: Key idea 在匀速圆周运动里，速度是切向的，而加速度和净力都指向圆心，大小分别是 v²/r 和 mv²/r。速率是恒定的；速度可不是。
cnpop.7: Trap 这套物理里压根没有什么向外的“离心力”，记牢别上当。没有任何东西把物体往圆心外推。乘客感觉到的那股“被甩出去”的劲，其实是座椅或车门在把他往里推、好让他乖乖留在圆周上，再加上他自己的惯性想让他一头扎着直线冲出去。永远别画一根向外的力的箭头，也永远别把向心力跟一个想象出来的向外的力划等号。
cnpop.8: Connects 向心力永远跟速度垂直，所以它做的功为零（功 = 力 × 沿力方向的位移）。这恰恰就是为什么匀速圆周运动的速率能稳住不变——净力只改运动方向，从不动动能。
cnpop.9: 一辆 1200 kg 的车以恒定 15 m s⁻¹ 的速率绕过一段半径 45 m 的平坦圆形弯道。求路面必须提供的摩擦力大小，以及能撑住这一切的最小摩擦系数。取 g = 9.8 m s⁻²。
cnpop.10: 公式：摩擦力是唯一的水平力，所以它就得扛起向心力，Fnet = mv²r。
cnpop.12: 答案：Fnet = 6.0 × 10³ N，水平指向弯道圆心。
cnpop.13: 要让路面掏得出这么大的力，可用的摩擦力 μmg 至少得有 6.0 × 10³ N。平坦路面上法向力 N = mg = 1200 × 9.8 = 1.18 × 10⁴ N，于是最小系数 μ = Fnet/N = 6.0 × 10³ ÷ 1.18 × 10⁴ = 0.51。要是实际能给的摩擦力比这小，那这车就压不住弯，会沿着切线方向往外滑出去。

cnpop.15: 把法向力拆成水平和竖直两个分量。水平分量 N sinθ 指向弯道圆心，扛起向心力。竖直分量 N cosθ 朝上，跟重力打平手，因为竖直方向没有加速度。把这两个条件都写出来：
cnpop.16: 拿第一个方程去除以第二个方程，N 和 m 当场消掉，因为 sinθ/cosθ = tanθ：
cnpop.17: 整理一下就得到向心加速度，以及那个设计速率——车在无摩擦倾斜面上完美过弯的那个唯一速率：
cnpop.18: 在设计速率下，结果只跟 r、g 和 θ 有关——跟车的质量半毛钱关系都没有。一辆大卡车和一辆摩托车，在同一个无摩擦倾斜面上是用同一个设计速率过弯的。
cnpop.19: Key idea 在无摩擦倾斜赛道上，单凭法向力的水平分量 N sinθ 就把向心力包圆了，而竖直分量 N cosθ 跟重力打平。由此得到 ac = g tanθ，以及设计速率 v = √(rg tanθ)。
cnpop.20: Trap 斜面上的法向力不等于 mg，这点千万别想当然。在倾斜面上它是 N = mg/cosθ，比 mg 还大。要把 N 拆成水平和竖直分量，每个方向单独列一个方程；别傻乎乎地假设法向力直接跟重力平衡。在设计速率下根本没有摩擦力——只有 N 的那两个分量在干活。速率高过设计速率时，车会想往斜面上方滑，摩擦力就沿斜面朝下指；低过设计速率时，车会想往下滑，摩擦力就沿斜面朝上指。
cnpop.21: 一条测试赛道跟水平方向倾斜 20°。赛道上有一段半径 80 m 的弯。求车不靠任何摩擦力过这个弯的设计速率，以及那个速率下的向心加速度。取 g = 9.8 m s⁻²。
cnpop.22: 公式：在设计速率下，单凭法向力的水平分量就扛起向心力，于是 v = √(rg tanθ)、ac = g tanθ。
cnpop.24: 答案：v = 16.9 m s⁻¹ ≈ 17 m s⁻¹，向心加速度为 3.6 m s⁻²，水平指向弯道圆心。车的质量压根没进这个计算。
cnpop.25: 当一个物体做竖直圆周运动时——绳上的小球、抡过头顶的一桶水、翻过一个鼓包或者俯冲过一个山谷的车——重力自始至终都是同一个套路（竖直向下），可它跟圆心的关系会随着位置一点点变。我们只需要分析两个极端点，最高点和最低点，在这两个点上，净力、重力和张力（或者法向力）全都落在过圆心的那条竖直线上。
cnpop.26: 在圆周顶部，圆心就在物体正下方。重力 mg 和绳中张力 T 全都朝下指——也就是朝圆心——所以它俩是相加的。沿圆心方向写牛顿第二定律，得到：
cnpop.27: 在圆周底部，圆心就在物体正上方。张力朝上、指向圆心，而重力朝下、背离圆心，所以它俩是相减的。取朝圆心（向上）为正方向：
cnpop.28: 所以同样的速率下，底部的张力比顶部的大，因为在底部张力既得托住重力，又得扛起向心力，一人干两份活。
cnpop.29: 物体在顶部附近越转越慢时，需要的张力就越来越少。顶部的最小速率就是绳子刚好绷不住、张力降到零、靠重力一个人撑起整份向心力的那个速率。在顶部的方程里令 T = 0：
cnpop.30: 低于这个速率，物体在顶部就维持不住圆周轨迹了，会往里掉。换成车的情况，是支撑面提供一个法向力 N 来顶替张力。翻过一个鼓包（向上凸的坡顶）时，圆心在车下方，所以 mg − N = mv²/r，得 N = m(g − v²/r)：路面往上顶的力比重力小，司机就觉得自己变轻了。在一个低洼处（向下凹的山谷）里，圆心在上方，所以 N − mg = mv²/r，得 N = m(g + v²/r)：路面往上顶的力比重力还狠，司机就觉得自己变沉了。当 v² = gr 时，过坡顶时法向力降到零，车就直接飞离路面了。
cnpop.31: Key idea 在竖直圆周的顶部，T 和 mg 都指向圆心，所以 T + mg = mv²/r。在底部它俩对着干，所以 T − mg = mv²/r。绳子在顶部刚好绷不住时 v = √(gr)。
cnpop.32: Trap 重力的正负号在这两个点之间会翻面，是因为圆心方向变了，可不是因为重力本身变了。重力永远是 mg 竖直向下。在顶部，“向下”就是朝圆心，所以重力加到张力上；在底部，“向下”是背离圆心，所以重力是减掉的。每次写 Fnet = mv²/r 之前，先把圆心朝哪个方向给我搞清楚，而且别想当然地以为那个最小速率条件在顶部以外的地方还能用。
cnpop.33: 一个 0.20 kg 的小球系在一根长 0.80 m 的绳子末端，在竖直平面内甩成圆圈。求绳子保持绷紧的前提下，小球在最高点能有的最小速率；以及当小球在最高点以 4.0 m s⁻¹ 运动时，那里的绳中张力。取 g = 9.8 m s⁻²。
cnpop.34: 公式（最小速率）：在顶部绳子刚好松弛，所以 T = 0、mg = mv²/r，得 vmin = √(gr)。
cnpop.36: 答案：vmin = 2.8 m s⁻¹。低于这个速率，绳子就松了，小球往里掉。
cnpop.37: 公式（4.0 m s⁻¹ 时的张力）：在顶部两个力都指向圆心，所以 T + mg = mv²/r，得 T = mv²r − mg。
cnpop.39: 答案：T = 2.0 N，沿绳子指向圆心。因为 4.0 m s⁻¹ 超过了 2.8 m s⁻¹ 这个最小速率，张力是正的，绳子稳稳绷着。
cnpop.40: 一颗卫星——不管是月球这种天然的，还是国际空间站这种人造的——都可以建模成做匀速圆周运动的物体。它绕着中心天体、以恒定速率、沿固定半径的轨道跑，所以圆周运动那套结论统统照搬：速度是切向的，大小为 v²/r 的加速度指向轨道圆心。给这份向心加速度买单的唯一一个力就是重力，但这个力和轨道之间的定量关系——牛顿万有引力定律、轨道半径与周期的关系——属于研究领域 2 的内容，会在“场”那一章里展开。这里我们纯把卫星当成圆周运动的一个例子来处理。
cnpop.41: 在 Fnet = mv²/r 和 a = v²/r 里用的那个半径，是从中心天体的中心量起的轨道半径，单位是米：
cnpop.42: 轨道跟卫星自己的质量无关。a = v²/r 和引力加速度都只跟轨道半径、中心天体的质量有关，所以两颗质量不同、却共用同一条轨道的卫星，速率、周期和加速度全都一模一样。一位宇航员松手放掉的一把扳手，会乖乖跟着旁边的空间站同步飞，谁也不甩开谁。
cnpop.43: 一颗在圆轨道上的卫星，虽然速率恒定，却一直在加速，因为它的运动方向时刻在变。这份加速度由牛顿第二定律来解释：一个不平衡的力——引力——朝圆心方向作用，而不平衡的力会沿着它自己的方向产生加速度。这可不是第一定律（说的是力平衡时的运动）能解释的，也不是第三定律（讲的是卫星反过来对地球施加的那个等大反向的力）能解释的。
cnpop.44: Key idea 把卫星建模成半径 r = RE + altitude（单位是米）的匀速圆周运动。轨道跟卫星的质量无关。卫星之所以加速，是因为有个不平衡的力——引力——朝圆心方向作用：这是牛顿第二定律。
cnpop.45: Trap 两个反复送命的丢分点。第一，轨道半径不是高度：400 km 的高度给出 r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 4.00 × 10⁵ = 6.77 × 10⁶ m，而不是 4.00 × 10⁵ m。永远记得加上 RE，并把高度从 km 换成 m。第二，“一颗在圆轨道上的卫星为什么在加速？”这个问题的答案是牛顿第二定律——一个不平衡的（净）力导致朝圆心方向的加速度——绝不是第一或第三定律。
cnpop.46: Connects 引力为什么恰好供出 mv²/r、以及轨道半径怎么把周期给定死，都来自研究领域 2 的牛顿万有引力定律。完整的轨道推导、地球同步卫星和表观失重，都在那里展开——见“场”那一章里的 Satellites in circular orbit。
cnpop.47: 国际空间站在 400 km 的高度上运行，轨道周期为 92 min。把它的运动建模成匀速圆周运动，求轨道半径、轨道速率和向心加速度。取 RE = 6.37 × 10⁶ m。
cnpop.48: 公式：r = RE + altitude，然后 v = 2πrT、a = v²r。
cnpop.49: 代入（半径）：r = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 4.00 × 10⁵ = 6.77 × 10⁶ m，其中高度从 400 km 换算成了 4.00 × 10⁵ m。周期 T = 92 × 60 = 5.52 × 10³ s。
cnpop.51: 答案：a = v²/r = (7.71 × 10³)² ÷ (6.77 × 10⁶) = 8.8 m s⁻²，指向地球中心。这个值正好等于该高度处的引力场强度，所以光靠引力就能维持这条轨道——而且这结果跟空间站的质量没有半点关系。

ai.0: Here’s the surprising thing: an object cruising at constant speed around a circle is accelerating at every single instant — not because it’s speeding up, but because its direction of motion never stops changing. And that’s really the whole topic in a nutshell. Every problem boils down to one question: which real force, pointing toward the centre, is doing the work of bending that motion into a circle?
ai.1: An object is in uniform circular motion when it loops around a circle of fixed radius at constant speed. Notice the careful wording: the speed stays the same, but the velocity doesn’t — and that distinction is everything. Velocity is a vector, and its direction is forever swinging around. At every point along the path the velocity is tangential: it runs along the circle, square to the radius. And because that direction is endlessly changing, the object is accelerating, even though it never once gets faster or slower.
ai.2: That acceleration aims straight at the centre of the circle. We call it the centripetal acceleration — the word literally means “centre-seeking” — and for an object moving at speed v around a circle of radius r, its size is:
ai.3: This acceleration is radial — it sits perpendicular to the velocity at every instant. Now, Newton’s second law won’t let a mass accelerate unless some unbalanced force is shoving it along, so there must be a net force pointing toward the centre. That net force has a name — the centripetal force:
ai.4: Here’s a point worth pinning down: “centripetal force” is not some brand-new kind of force, and it never shows up in a free-body diagram as its own little arrow. It’s simply the label we hang on the resultant of the real forces — gravity, tension, friction, the normal force — once you’ve added them all up. So in every problem your very first job is to work out which real force, or which component of a real force, points toward the centre and therefore delivers the mv²/r.
ai.5: For a car driving around a flat circular road, that job falls to friction between the tyres and the road, pulling horizontally toward the centre of the bend. For an object whirled on a horizontal string, it’s the horizontal component of the tension in the string. In both cases the net force, the acceleration and the radius all line up along the same direction — inward, toward the centre.
ai.6: Key idea In uniform circular motion the velocity points tangentially, while the acceleration and the net force both aim toward the centre, with sizes v²/r and mv²/r. The speed holds steady; the velocity does not.
ai.7: Trap There is no outward “centrifugal force” lurking in this physics — nothing is pushing the object away from the centre. That outward feeling a passenger swears they feel? It’s really the seat or the door pressing them inward to keep them on the circle, combined with their own inertia trying to carry them straight ahead. So never draw an outward force arrow, and never set the centripetal force equal to some imagined outward force.
ai.8: Connects The centripetal force always sits perpendicular to the velocity, which means it does zero work (work = force × displacement along the force). And that’s exactly why the speed in uniform circular motion never budges — the net force keeps redirecting the motion, but it never adds or drains a scrap of kinetic energy.
ai.9: A 1200 kg car rounds a flat circular bend of radius 45 m at a constant speed of 15 m s⁻¹. Determine the magnitude of the friction force the road must provide, and the minimum coefficient of friction that makes this possible. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
ai.10: Formula: friction is the only horizontal force in play, so it’s the one supplying the centripetal force, Fnet = mv²r.
ai.12: Answer: Fnet = 6.0 × 10³ N, directed horizontally toward the centre of the bend.
ai.13: For the road to deliver this, the friction it has on offer, μmg, must be at least 6.0 × 10³ N. On a flat road the normal force is just N = mg = 1200 × 9.8 = 1.18 × 10⁴ N, so the smallest coefficient that works out is μ = Fnet/N = 6.0 × 10³ ÷ 1.18 × 10⁴ = 0.51. If the friction actually available falls short of this, the car simply can’t hold the bend — it slides outward along a tangent.
ai.14: A road or track is banked when its surface is tilted inward, toward the centre of the bend. The clever part is that banking lets a vehicle round the bend without leaning on friction at all. On a frictionless banked surface only two forces touch the vehicle: its weight mg, pulling straight down, and the normal force N, pushing perpendicular to the tilted surface. And because the surface is tipped by angle θ from the horizontal, the normal force is likewise tipped by θ away from the vertical.
ai.15: Now break the normal force into horizontal and vertical pieces. The horizontal component, N sinθ, points toward the centre of the bend and hands over the centripetal force. The vertical component, N cosθ, points upward and balances the weight, since there’s no vertical acceleration to account for. Writing down both conditions:
ai.16: Divide the first equation by the second and both N and m vanish, since sinθ/cosθ = tanθ:
ai.17: Rearrange, and out drops the centripetal acceleration along with the design speed — the one and only speed at which a vehicle threads the frictionless bank perfectly:
ai.18: At the design speed the answer leans only on r, g and θ — never on the mass of the vehicle. A truck and a motorcycle take the very same frictionless bank at the very same design speed.
ai.19: Key idea On a frictionless banked track the horizontal component of the normal force, N sinθ, single-handedly supplies the centripetal force, while the vertical component N cosθ balances the weight. That gives you ac = g tanθ and the design speed v = √(rg tanθ).
ai.20: Trap The normal force on a slope is not equal to mg. On a bank it works out to N = mg/cosθ, which is bigger than mg. Resolve N into horizontal and vertical components and write a separate equation for each direction — don’t fall into assuming the normal force balances the weight head-on. At the design speed there’s no friction at all; only the components of N are doing anything. Go above the design speed and the vehicle starts sliding up the bank, so friction points down the slope; drop below it and the vehicle slips down, so friction points up the slope.
ai.21: A test track is banked at 20° to the horizontal. A bend on the track has radius 80 m. Determine the design speed at which a vehicle rounds this bend with no reliance on friction, and the centripetal acceleration at that speed. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
ai.22: Formula: at the design speed the horizontal component of the normal force does the whole job of supplying the centripetal force, giving v = √(rg tanθ) and ac = g tanθ.
ai.23: Substitution: ac = 9.8 × tan20° = 9.8 × 0.364 = 3.57 m s⁻², and v = √(80 × 9.8 × tan20°) = √(80 × 9.8 × 0.364) = √285
ai.24: Answer: v = 16.9 m s⁻¹ ≈ 17 m s⁻¹, with a centripetal acceleration of 3.6 m s⁻² pointing horizontally toward the centre of the bend. Notice the mass of the vehicle never even shows up in the calculation.
ai.25: When an object loops around a vertical circle — a ball on a string, a bucket of water swung overhead, a vehicle cresting a hump or plunging through a valley — gravity behaves the same way the whole time (straight down), but its relationship to the centre of the circle shifts from point to point. Happily, you only need to analyse the two extremes, the highest point and the lowest point, where the net force, the weight and the tension (or normal force) all fall neatly along the vertical line through the centre.
ai.26: At the top of the circle the centre sits directly below the object. Both the weight mg and the string tension T point downward — toward the centre — so they team up and add. Newton’s second law toward the centre then gives:
ai.27: At the bottom of the circle the centre is now directly above the object. The tension pulls upward toward the centre while the weight pulls downward away from it, so this time they fight and subtract. Taking toward the centre (upward) as positive:
ai.28: So the tension at the bottom comes out greater than at the top for the same speed — because down there the tension has to pull double duty: holding up the weight and supplying the centripetal force.
ai.29: As the object slows near the top, it needs less and less tension. The minimum speed at the top is the speed at which the string just goes slack: the tension drops to zero and gravity alone is left to supply the centripetal force. Set T = 0 in the top equation:
ai.30: Go any slower than this and the object can’t hold the circular path at the top — it falls inward. For a vehicle, the supporting surface stands in for tension by providing a normal force N. Crossing a hump (a convex crest), the centre is below the vehicle, so mg − N = mv²/r, giving N = m(g − v²/r): the road pushes up with less than the weight, and the driver feels lighter. In a dip (a concave valley) the centre is above, so N − mg = mv²/r, giving N = m(g + v²/r): the road pushes up harder than the weight, and the driver feels heavier. The normal force drops to zero, and the vehicle leaves the road, when v² = gr over the crest.
ai.31: Key idea At the top of a vertical circle both T and mg point toward the centre, so T + mg = mv²/r. At the bottom they oppose, so T − mg = mv²/r. The string just goes slack at the top when v = √(gr).
ai.32: Trap The sign of the weight flips between the two points because the centre lies in a different direction — not because gravity itself changes. Gravity is always mg straight down, full stop. At the top “down” happens to be toward the centre, so the weight adds to the tension; at the bottom “down” is away from the centre, so the weight subtracts. Always settle which way the centre lies before you write Fnet = mv²/r, and never assume the minimum-speed condition applies anywhere but the top.
ai.33: A 0.20 kg ball on the end of a string of length 0.80 m is swung in a vertical circle. Determine the minimum speed the ball can have at the highest point while the string stays taut, and the string tension at the highest point when the ball moves there at 4.0 m s⁻¹. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻².
ai.34: Formula (minimum speed): at the top the string is right on the edge of going slack, so T = 0 and mg = mv²/r, giving vmin = √(gr).
ai.36: Answer: vmin = 2.8 m s⁻¹. Drop below this speed and the string goes slack — the ball falls inward.
ai.37: Formula (tension at 4.0 m s⁻¹): at the top both forces point toward the centre, so T + mg = mv²/r, giving T = mv²r − mg.
ai.39: Answer: T = 2.0 N, directed along the string toward the centre. Since 4.0 m s⁻¹ comfortably beats the minimum speed of 2.8 m s⁻¹, the tension comes out positive and the string stays taut.
ai.40: A satellite — whether a natural one like the Moon or an artificial one like the International Space Station — can be modelled as an object in uniform circular motion. It circles the central body at constant speed on a path of fixed radius, so everything you’ve built up for circular motion carries straight over: the velocity is tangential, and the acceleration of size v²/r points toward the centre of the orbit. The single force supplying that centripetal acceleration is gravity — but the quantitative link between that force and the orbit (Newton’s law of universal gravitation and how orbital radius ties to period) belongs to Area of Study 2 and gets developed in the Fields chapter. Here we treat the satellite purely as a case of circular motion.
ai.41: The radius you feed into Fnet = mv²/r and a = v²/r is the orbital radius measured from the centre of the central body, in metres:
ai.42: The orbit is independent of the satellite’s mass. Both a = v²/r and the gravitational acceleration ride entirely on the orbital radius and the central mass, so two satellites of different mass sharing one orbit move at the same speed, period and acceleration. A spanner an astronaut lets go keeps pace right alongside the station.
ai.43: A satellite in a circular orbit is accelerating — even with its speed dead constant — because its direction of motion is always turning. That acceleration is the work of Newton’s second law: an unbalanced force (gravity) acts toward the centre, and an unbalanced force produces an acceleration in its own direction. It’s not the first law (which describes motion when forces are balanced) and it’s not the third law (which is about the equal and opposite force the satellite exerts back on Earth).
ai.44: Key idea Model a satellite as uniform circular motion of radius r = RE + altitude (in metres). The orbit is independent of the satellite’s mass. The satellite accelerates because an unbalanced force — gravity — acts toward the centre: this is Newton’s second law.
ai.45: Trap Two mark-losers crop up again and again. First, the orbital radius is not the altitude: an altitude of 400 km gives r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 4.00 × 10⁵ = 6.77 × 10⁶ m, not 4.00 × 10⁵ m. Always add RE and convert the altitude from km to m. Second, “why is a satellite in a circular orbit accelerating?” is answered by Newton’s second law — an unbalanced (net) force causes acceleration toward the centre — never the first or third law.
ai.46: Connects The reason gravity supplies exactly mv²/r, and how the orbital radius locks in the period, comes from Newton’s law of universal gravitation in Area of Study 2. The full orbit derivation, geostationary satellites and apparent weightlessness all get developed there — see Satellites in circular orbit in the Fields chapter.
ai.47: The International Space Station orbits at an altitude of 400 km with an orbital period of 92 min. Modelling its motion as uniform circular motion, determine the orbital radius, the orbital speed and the centripetal acceleration. Take RE = 6.37 × 10⁶ m.
ai.48: Formula: r = RE + altitude, then v = 2πrT and a = v²r.
ai.49: Substitution (radius): r = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 4.00 × 10⁵ = 6.77 × 10⁶ m, with the altitude converted from 400 km to 4.00 × 10⁵ m. The period is T = 92 × 60 = 5.52 × 10³ s.
ai.51: Answer: a = v²/r = (7.71 × 10³)² ÷ (6.77 × 10⁶) = 8.8 m s⁻², directed toward Earth’s centre. This matches the gravitational field strength at that altitude — which is precisely why gravity alone keeps the orbit going — and the result, notice, doesn’t depend on the station’s mass.
